entry.title: How Much of Your Post Does AI Search Actually Grab? Breaking Down Claude's WebSearch and WebFetch
entry.content:
Intro

A while back I wrote Is SEO Not Enough? Meet AEO — Getting Your Site Found by AI Search, and right after finishing it a thought hit me: when AI does a web search, how much of my blog does it actually grab? The whole article verbatim? The first 500 characters? Or does it bail after seeing just the title? So I dug into it a bit, and this post walks through Anthropic’s official web_search and web_fetch tool docs, runs a quick test against my own blog, and ends with what all this concretely means for how you should write posts and copy.

First things first — “search” and “fetch” are two different things

Before we go any further, there’s one thing I need to make crystal clear: when AI runs a query, “search” and “fetching the page body” are not the same operation. They’re two separate things, handled as two distinct stages.

  • Stage 1 (WebSearch): the AI takes your question and calls the WebSearch tool (which hits a search engine). What comes back is a list of search results — each entry has things like URL and title, but no page body.
  • Stage 2 (WebFetch): after looking at the search result list, the AI decides which entries are worth digging into, then fires a WebFetch request at each of those URLs that basically says “give me the body of this page.” That’s when the actual content gets pulled in.

Wondering why it doesn’t just grab the body during the search stage?

The biggest reason is Context (context window) limits. Think about it — if every search shoved 10 results’ worth of full bodies in, your usable Context would blow up fast, and then you’d start complaining the AI is dumb and forgets what you just asked it (because, well, the Context overflowed). That’s why it’s split into two stages: search first to get a “list”, then decide which entries from that list to actually fetch.

I drew up a flow diagram to make the timing easier to picture:

Now that the two-stage split makes sense, let’s look at what each stage actually pulls in.

What does WebSearch pull in?

Going straight to Anthropic’s official web_search tool docs — every search result entry has only four fields:

  • url: the page URL
  • title: the page title
  • page_age: when the page was last updated
  • encrypted_content: encrypted content, not for the AI to read the article — it’s for multi-turn conversation citations

Yep, that’s it. Four fields.

What the AI sees during the search stage (WebSearch) is “URL, title, last updated” — three pieces of human-readable info. No body content at all.

What if the AI cites your content? There’s a cap on that too:

Each web_search_result_location‘s cited_text is up to 150 characters of the cited content

In short: at most 150 characters of quoted text. And that’s just the API-level spec.

Claude Code’s built-in WebSearch shaves it down further. According to Mikhail Shilkov’s breakdown of Claude Code’s internal behavior, Claude Code even drops page_age and encrypted_content, keeping only title and url.

So basically… at the search stage the AI sees nothing more than one title and one URL from your site. That’s it.

What does WebFetch pull in?

Now for when the body actually gets pulled — Stage 2, WebFetch, as mentioned above.

Once the AI has the search results, if it decides to open up a few entries, it fires one WebFetch request per URL, and that’s when the full body comes back. How much of it?

This needs to be split into two layers, because the API and Claude Code work differently.

Note
When I say “API” here, I mean the Anthropic API’s web_fetch tool. “Claude Code” means the WebFetch feature built into Anthropic’s product. The two have different specs and flows.

API-level web_fetch

The Anthropic API’s web_fetch tool has a parameter called max_content_tokens that developers can set themselves — though the official docs use 100,000 tokens in their examples.

The docs also give a reference conversion:

Content sizeEstimated tokens
Average web page 10 KB~2,500 tokens
Large doc page 100 KB~25,000 tokens
Research paper PDF 500 KB~125,000 tokens

So a medium-length Chinese blog post in plain text is usually 1–2,000 tokens, way below the 100K ceiling. Truncation basically isn’t a concern unless you wrote a 50,000-character monster (like Dream of the Red Chamber or something).

One thing to note though: web_fetch’s citation works differently from web_search. It uses start_char_index / end_char_index to pick out a specific position in the article (although the docs don’t pin down a hard character limit).

Claude Code’s built-in WebFetch

Claude Code’s built-in WebFetch goes a different route.

Per Mikhail Shilkov’s breakdown, the WebFetch flow is:

  1. Convert HTML to Markdown using the Turndown package
  2. Extract the first 100 KB of plain text
  3. Pass that 100 KB to the Haiku 3.5 model
  4. Haiku summarizes the answer based on your prompt and returns the summary to the main model

The real kicker is step 3. You’ll notice the main model (the Claude model you’re actually using) never sees the page’s original text — it only sees the version Haiku summarized. Which means what your writing turns into by the time it reaches the main model is decided by how Haiku reads it, not by how much you wrote.

The citation has a limit too. The rule Mikhail extracted from Claude Code’s internal prompt is “Enforce a strict 125-character maximum for quotes from any source document” — so quotes max out at 125 characters.

You’re probably wondering — so how much is 100 KB of plain text? For Chinese, where each character is roughly 3 bytes, 100 KB fits 30,000+ characters; for English at 1 byte per character, that’s well over 100,000 characters. No regular blog post is going to hit that ceiling. So the real concern should be “how do I get Haiku to extract the parts I want to be quoted,” not “how much content am I feeding Haiku.”

Running a real test against my own blog

Enough theory — let’s actually test it.

I had Claude call the WebFetch tool and pointed it at my blog’s homepage (israynotarray.com) to grab the body.

How do you actually test this? You can lean on Hooks (article in Mandarin) to log every stage’s input and output, which makes it clear exactly what the AI pulled at each step.

Here’s my Hook config — this way every WebSearch and WebFetch I/O gets logged:

The prompt I used:

The actual Input was:

After it ran, the WebFetch result — the summary that came back — looked roughly like this:

See it? What the AI actually pulled from my blog is just this tiny under-1000-character summary. A whole website obviously contains way more than this — the rest was never seen by the AI. The Haiku middle layer reads the page and only extracts what it judges relevant to my prompt. If I opened the same page in a browser, I’d see a full grid of article cards plus a sidebar — but Haiku doesn’t ship the full grid back.

I also tried an older post with a deliberately broken URL path, and got this:

Even the content of your 404 page is invisible to the AI — WebFetch just reports back the 404 Not Found error and the AI has no way to see what your 404 page says. Which means if your site has any path issues, you’ve refactored URLs, or you only have frontend routing without real pages, the AI can’t pull anything.

Side note — this lines up with a caveat in Claude’s official docs:

The web fetch tool currently does not support websites dynamically rendered with JavaScript.

If your blog is built on a frontend SPA and the content is entirely rendered by JavaScript runtime, what the AI grabs might just be empty-shell HTML with no articles visible at all. Static generators (Hexo, Astro, Next.js in SSG mode) are relatively safe, since the build output is fully rendered HTML — the AI grabs and immediately sees content (welcome to the Hexo club).

Don’t forget the robots.txt layer

There’s one more important piece — whether the AI can pull your site has a major prerequisite: robots.txt.

AI crawlers basically split into two types: search-style (cite and link back to your site) and training-style (eat content to feed the model, not necessarily linking back). The common mapping looks like this:

CrawlerTypeBehavior
Claude-SearchBot / Claude-UserSearchReal-time fetch when Claude answers, cites back
ClaudeBotTrainingFetches content to feed Claude training
OAI-SearchBot / ChatGPT-UserSearchReal-time fetch when ChatGPT answers, cites back
GPTBotTrainingFetches content to feed GPT training
PerplexityBotSearchUsed by Perplexity engine, cites back
Google-ExtendedTrainingFor Gemini training
CCBotTrainingCommon Crawl public dataset

If you want to be cited by AI but don’t want your content used for training, the most common strategy is “allow search-style, block training-style.”

Here’s a robots.txt template you can copy-paste:

For more detail on writing robots.txt, you can check out my earlier post A quick guide to robots.txt (article in Mandarin). For a full Agent Readiness setup to score 100, see From 3 to 100! How to Get Your Site to Pass isitagentready’s AI Agent Readiness Check.

So what does this concretely mean for writing?

After all of that, what does this actually mean for how you write? It means a lot, actually. Once you understand the constraints above, there are four things you can specifically work on.

Titles need to stand on their own

At the search stage, all the AI sees about your article is two fields — title and URL.

If your title needs a subtitle or context to make sense, when the AI lines it up against ten other results it’ll get skipped.

A quick comparison:

  • Weaker: “Implementation Notes”
  • Stronger: “Complete Implementation Notes: Content Negotiation for HTML-to-Markdown in a Cloudflare Worker”

The stronger version packs in “topic, tool, what it does, and article type” — the AI doesn’t even need to open the page to know whether it’s worth fetching.

Lead with the answer

At the WebFetch stage, the Haiku middle layer reads top-down. The first 300–500 characters decide what it summarizes back. If your opening is “Before we get into X, let’s recap a bit of history…”, Haiku reads halfway through and discovers the intro is all background and no answer — so it just summarizes the background.

The right move is to make the first sentence of every H2 a direct conclusion, then add the context after. I covered this principle in Is SEO Not Enough? Meet AEO — Getting Your Site Found by AI Search too — worth reading alongside this.

Design single sentences that can be quoted standalone

cited_text is 150 characters on web_search and 125 characters on Claude Code’s built-in WebFetch. That means when the AI quotes you, the slot it has is one short sentence that “makes sense without context.”

Consciously design sentences like that. For example:

  • Weaker: “This is a bit different from the method mentioned earlier — the main difference is…” (makes no sense without context)
  • Stronger: “llms.txt was proposed by Answer.AI co-founder Jeremy Howard in 2024, with the goal of proactively telling AI what important content a site has.” (stands on its own without surrounding text)

After writing a paragraph, pick one sentence and ask yourself: if someone who hadn’t read the rest of the post saw just this sentence, would they get it? If yes, it has a shot at being quoted.

Use structure as Haiku’s navigation markers

H2, H3, - lists, tables, ```js code blocks — these Markdown structures are especially useful for the middleware summary layer. When Haiku reads the Markdown converted from your HTML, it treats headings as “what this section is about” indexes, lists as “main points” signals, and tables as “supporting data” units.

If your whole article is pure prose paragraphs, Haiku has no markers and has to grind through it semantically — what comes out is scattered. If you have clear structure, Haiku can summarize along the markers, and the result lines up with the points you actually want quoted.

Wrap-up

To bring it all together.

So how much of your blog does AI search actually pull?

The answer breaks down into three layers:

  • Search stage: title + URL only
  • Body fetch stage: the API default can fit your whole article, but Claude Code goes through Haiku summarization with a 100 KB cut-off
  • Citation stage: web_search is 150 characters, Claude Code WebFetch is 125 characters

Writing for AI search means targeting those three gates — it’s not about getting the AI to memorize your entire post.

Hopefully this gave you a more concrete sense of how much AI search actually pulls from your blog.

If your blog hasn’t set up AI bot routing yet, copy the robots.txt template above to get the basics in place — the rest is just content over time.
entry.summary: This post digs into how much of your blog AI actually pulls in when it does a web search. I went through Anthropic's official web_search and web_fetch tool specs, broke down the fact that the search stage only returns title + URL, the 150-character cited_text cap, and Claude Code's built-in WebFetch using Haiku 3.5 to summarize a 100 KB truncation, then ran a real test against my own blog. Wraps up with four actionable AEO writing tips: titles that carry their own context, lead with the answer, design single sentences that can be quoted standalone, and use structure as navigation markers.

entry.title: How to Use Gemini Omni Video Generation: 30 Prompt Templates for Reels, Product Demos, and Logo Reveals
entry.content:
Intro

Honestly, I’ve been pretty skeptical of AI generation for a while. Fingers, on-screen text, audio — these are the details AI has always tripped on, and the output usually screams “yeah, this is AI” instead of looking like something actually shot on camera.

But I spent some time with Gemini Omni recently and… the improvement is real. The overall picture quality is pretty impressive. Some small details still aren’t perfect, but it’s hit that fuzzy zone between “this is clearly AI” and “this could be real footage” — especially this clip I generated the other day:

A bunch of friends saw it and were honestly thrown — they thought I’d actually shot a promo for “Code Review of My Youth” myself ( not happening ). So I’d say Gemini Omni has made some real, tangible progress on the “looks shot” front.

If you’re not totally familiar with prompt structure or how to interact with an LLM, you can flip through my earlier post on LLM, RAG, Prompt — A Look at the Common GenAI Vocabulary first. This one will jump straight into Gemini Omni.

This post breaks into four parts: first clearing up a common mix-up (Gemini Omni in the Gemini App vs. Veo 3.1 on the developer side — not the same thing), then what makes Omni stronger than Veo, then how to actually write prompts that hold up, and finally 30 scenario-specific templates — IG Reels hooks, product demos, Logo Reveals, Cinematic B-roll, Before-After transitions, knowledge-deck animations, quote cards, and Lifestyle / travel / food cuts — all copy-paste ready.

First, let’s clear this up: video in the Gemini App — is it Omni or Veo?

A lot of people ask “where did Veo go?” — and honestly the confusion makes sense. Early 2026 the video generation inside the Gemini App actually was Veo 3.1. But after the May I/O it all got swapped over.

In short:

  • Gemini Omni is the new unified multimodal model Google launched at I/O 2026 (May). It’s now the main thing powering video generation inside the Gemini App. 10-second clips, 9:16 or 16:9, native synced audio, accepts a mix of text + image + video + audio as input.
  • Veo 3.1 / Veo 3.1 Lite didn’t go away — they just got reassigned. They’ve been pushed to the developer side and are mainly available through the Gemini API, Vertex AI, and Google AI Studio, for engineers building products against the API or running large batch generations.

If I had to put it in everyday terms — Gemini Omni is more like a fully automatic camera: you tell it what to shoot and it handles the aperture and shutter itself. Veo 3.1 is more like a manual camera: more control, but you have to know what you’re doing. For most people hopping into the Gemini App to generate a Reels clip, an IG short, or a Logo Reveal, Omni is enough.

What makes Gemini Omni strong?

For me personally, four things about Omni actually stand out: native synced audio, multimodal mixed input, Chinese text rendering finally being stable, and conversational editing. Walking through each one.

1. Native synced audio

The most painful thing about AI video used to be the audio, not the picture.

The picture would come out okay, but you’d have to hunt down voice / SFX / ambient separately on Pixabay or layer them in your editor afterwards.

Omni just swallows that part. You write things like “Dialogue: the female lead says ‘we have to leave now’”, “SFX: distant thunder”, “Ambient: low murmur of a late-night café” into your prompt, and it generates all three audio layers synced to the picture. Lip-sync lines up, SFX timing hits the right beats (yeah, sometimes things slip slightly, but it’s hard to tell now).

2. Multimodal mixed input

It’s not just text descriptions.

You can hand it a photo and say “make this photo come alive — coffee steaming, someone walking past the window”, and it’ll use that photo as the first frame and extend forward. You can also hand it an existing video and say “same scene but switch to dusk lighting” — that’s a style transfer. Image, audio, video, text can all be mixed in as input.

In my own case, I just handed Omni the cover of one of my books, and it dropped the cover straight into the video where the book was supposed to appear. That way I don’t have to re-describe what the cover looks like in every prompt, and it’s more accurate too.

3. Chinese text rendering is finally there

This is the same lineage as the leap Images 2.0 made on static images (if you haven’t seen what Images 2.0 can do on static images, take a quick look at How to Use GPT-5.5 and ChatGPT Images 2.0: 30 Prompt Templates for IG Cards, Knowledge Decks, and Product Mockups).

Omni brings that same capability over to motion. Chinese subtitles, opening titles, logo text in videos all come out correctly. Occasionally a character will still slip, but it’s hit the threshold of actually being usable.

4. Conversational editing

This is the one I personally find most useful. If a clip comes out wrong, you don’t have to rewrite the whole prompt — you can just say “change the lighting to warm dusk colors, keep everything else” in the next turn, and it’ll only touch that part. Really helpful for series content (same character, same style, different action) — you don’t have to re-describe every detail each time.

For me, the difference this round isn’t about “drawing more artistically” — it’s about “looking like it was actually shot”.

Post-production (pacing, captions, brand colors) still has to happen, but the “shooting” part has basically been eaten 80% by AI (probably?).

A universal structure for writing video prompts

Before you start copy-pasting templates, a quick note on the overall structure — otherwise you’ll just swap topics blindly and the output quality keeps bouncing around.

After working through the official prompt guide and a fair bit of trial-and-error, for the prompt to hold up these six dimensions all need to be described:

DimensionWhat it coversExamples
Composition / cameraHow the shot is framed, how the camera moveswide shot, close-up, dolly forward, orbit, POV, handheld
StyleThe overall aesthetic directioncinematic, IG vlog, anime, Pixar, documentary, studio
LightingLight source, color temperature, qualitygolden hour, neon, natural window light, soft studio, backlight
SceneLocation and background objectswooden table, café, Taipei street, minimalist white background
ActionWhat the subject is doing and how it moveswalking past, turning, reaching for a cup, hitting the keyboard
On-screen textText that appears on screensubtitle content, position, entrance animation

On top of those six, video has two extra dimensions that static images don’t — timing and audio. Those need their own slot:

ItemNotes
Timeline / TimestampFor multi-shot, use [00:00-00:03] to mark which segment happens when
AudioDialogue, SFX, Ambient — label all three layers separately

Note
Two things are outside your prompt’s control: aspect ratio (16:9 / 9:16) is selected in the Gemini App UI, and length is currently capped at 10 seconds by Google (their stated reason is a deliberate product decision, not a model limitation).
So you won’t see “please generate an X-second clip” in any of the 30 templates below. Pick the aspect ratio based on the scene — talking head and Reels-style go 9:16, cinematic and product demo go 16:9.

In short, don’t let the model guess. The more specific you are, the more precisely it generates.

Note
When you write Chinese dialogue, explicitly specify “clear Mandarin female voice (Taiwanese accent)” or “natural Mandarin male voice (Taiwanese)” in the prompt. Otherwise the voice occasionally drifts into an unintended accent or heavily English-leaning Mandarin — this is just a function of how the training data is distributed.

If you’re editing an existing video (local change instead of full regen), add a “preserve” vs “change” pair to your prompt:

The logic of this structure is “which parts of this clip do I want untouched, which parts do I want changed” — that way the model only modifies what you asked for and leaves the rest as-is.

30 recommended prompt templates

30 prompts organized into eight categories, all copy-paste ready.

For each one I’ll briefly explain the use case, then give the prompt body, and below that the video I actually got from running it. Swap whatever’s in [ ] brackets for your own topic.

Note
The example videos below were generated using the Chinese versions of these prompts (partly the point — to show off Omni’s Chinese rendering). The English templates here produce the same layouts with English text. Mirror the field names (“Composition:”, “Style:”, etc.) when you write your own.

Note
Video prompts work best in the 20–50 word range (or equivalent in Chinese) — too short and the model has nothing to go on, too long and the key points get diluted. I’ve tried to keep each template below in that range.

1. IG Reels / TikTok hooks

These are the clips that grab people in the first 2 seconds while they’re scrolling Reels. The key is to throw the conflict, question, or contrast out immediately — don’t waste time on setup.

1. Question-style hook (subject talking to camera)

The most direct opener — main character looking into the camera, asking a question that makes you want to keep watching.

2. Third-person narrator hook

No real face on screen — just hand-drawn or symbolic close-up with narration. Good for creators who don’t want to show their face.

3. Before / After two-second switch opener

Compress the contrast straight into the first two or three seconds — strong visual impact.

4. Countdown opener

A countdown naturally creates “what’s about to happen?” tension — great for listicle-style content.

5. POV scenario hook

First-person POV pulls viewers straight into the scene — maximum immersion.

2. Product / service demos

These are what I reach for most when making marketing assets, Side Project landing page heroes, or product intro videos. Key things: clean interface, tight pacing.

6. App UI walkthrough demo

Compress the most important user action into 8 seconds — good for IG ads or App Store previews.

7. SaaS Landing Hero animated background

The animated background for a Hero section, giving off “this product is sleek” energy.

8. Product mockup loop (360° rotation)

Standard for e-commerce product pages and IG product Reels — this one should be a seamless loop.

9. Service flow animation (explainer)

Turn an abstract service flow into something visual — good for the “How it works” section of a landing page.

3. Logo reveal / brand motion

Brand motion clips for the open or close of a video — 3 to 6 seconds is the sweet spot. Pacing has to be crisp, don’t let it drag.

10. Classic logo reveal

The safest version — works for pretty much any context.

11. Liquid metal logo reveal

Heavier-texture version — good fit for luxury brands or tech companies.

12. Hand-drawn ink logo

Good fit for indie/lifestyle brands or solo studios.

4. Cinematic B-roll

B-roll is the stuff you layer under your main footage to carry pacing. Omni is strong here — it understands cinematography language, so words like anamorphic, dolly forward, shallow depth of field all land.

13. City night drone shot

Great for vlog openers or social previews — instantly gives a sense of story.

14. Slow-motion raindrop close-up

The kind of shot you see in calming content, ASMR, or skincare ads.

15. Coffee brewing process

A go-to lifestyle B-roll — works for cafés, brand ads, indie vlogs.

16. Time-lapse city dusk

Time-lapse is a great fit for Omni — it can directly generate the kind of light-change content that’s inherently temporal.

5. Knowledge-deck animations

If you’ve made IG knowledge decks before, you know the format. Animating them usually bumps engagement another step up.

17. Concept card flip

Compress a term explanation into a “question on the front, answer on the back” flip animation.

18. Three-step flow animation

Animated version of “here’s how you do it, in three steps”.

19. Comparison table reveal

Two things side by side, revealed row by row — holds viewer attention better than a static graphic.

6. Before / After transitions

Contrast content tends to do well on Reels — people are wired to be interested in “change”.

20. Room makeover Before / After

Standard format for home, organization, glow-up content.

21. Code refactor Before / After

Engineering audiences eat this up — make the abstract result of a refactor visible.

22. Copy rewrite Before / After

Beloved by anyone doing copywriting / content teaching.

7. Quote card animations

Animated quote cards tend to hold attention longer than static images in a busy feed.

23. Quote card animation

Design / product / leadership quotes tend to get shared a lot.

Note
Gemini Omni blocks the likenesses and names of real public figures outright (politicians, tech founders, celebrities, athletes — same story across Veo / Sora / Midjourney and every other mainstream tool, since this is the standard platform policy against real-person likeness misuse). For quote / tribute style content, use a fictional name, anonymize, or just put your own name. In the example below I just used my own.

24. Short Chinese quote card

Japanese-minimal aesthetic — the kind of thing that gets bookmarked on IG.

25. Story-style page-flip quote

Two or three quotes in sequence, mimicking a page-flip — good for IG Story ads or brand storytelling.

8. Lifestyle / travel / food cuts

This kind of content tends to get the most consistent reach on IG Reels and TikTok — pacing and atmosphere matter most. Omni’s ambient audio really shines here.

26. Morning ritual vlog cut

A go-to opener for home / lifestyle vlogs.

27. Food plating close-up

Essential for recipe content, restaurant ads, food IG accounts.

28. One-day travel cut

A quick-cut day-in-the-life edit — a standard format for travel accounts.

29. Pet daily life close-up

Calming content — a go-to for cat / dog accounts.

30. City walk-and-talk

Common camera language for interviews, documentaries, podcast trailers.

Practical tips

Knowing the structure and templates isn’t quite enough — here are a few tips that have actually saved me time.

1. When Chinese fails (subtitles or accent)

If only a character or two in the subtitles slip, don’t regenerate the whole thing. Just go back to the chat in the Gemini App and say…

It’ll touch only that spot.

If the entire subtitle is garbled, it’s almost always because the prompt didn’t put the text in quotes — go back and wrap it in 「」 or 『』 and try again. Way more stable.

As for accent — Chinese dialogue occasionally drifts into an unintended accent or English-leaning Mandarin. Adding an explicit “clear Mandarin female voice (Taiwanese accent)” or “natural Mandarin male voice (Taiwanese)” to the prompt visibly improves the accuracy.

2. For seamless loop videos

For product mockups, logo backgrounds, SaaS hero motion — anything meant to loop endlessly — the prompt has to explicitly say “loops seamlessly” and “first and last frame should align”. The action also has to be periodic (rotate once, cycle through a motion, etc.) — don’t write something one-off like “a coffee cup gets knocked over”.

3. Aspect ratio per platform

Different platforms want different aspect ratios. Remember to set this in the Gemini App UI before generating (it only offers 16:9 and 9:16 — anything else you’ll have to crop after). Otherwise the output may not be directly usable. Common ratios:

Use caseRatio
IG Reels / TikTok / YouTube Shorts9:16 (portrait)
IG feed video1:1 (square) or 4:5 (portrait)
YouTube main video / ads16:9 (landscape)
Blog embed / OG animation preview16:9
Facebook feed1:1 or 16:9

You can see from this table that most short-form content is now 9:16 portrait. For Reels, set 9:16 in the App first; for YouTube main videos, set 16:9 — don’t generate everything at 16:9 and then crop to portrait, the framing gets thrown off.

4. Don’t cram too many actions into one shot

Honestly, 10 seconds is pretty limited.

If your prompt tries to do “walks through the door + sits down + opens laptop + starts typing + sips coffee” all at once, the model can’t fit it in, and usually drops motion smoothness or object consistency at the end.

A safe ceiling is 1–2 main actions per clip. If you need more, split using timestamp and use multiple shots — or just generate two separate clips and cut them together.

Wrap-up

So to wrap up — this post covers how Gemini Omni and Veo 3.1 split the work on the Gemini App side, what makes Omni strong, the prompt structure, and 30 scenario-specific prompt templates.

Honestly, before Omni I always thought AI video was a toy. Fine for concept demos or generative art, but if you wanted to actually use it for Reels, product demos, or Logo Reveals, there was always something off — janky motion, garbled text, or audio you had to source separately.

Omni is the first version that made me feel “okay, this is actually usable straight out of the box” (probably).

There are still limits, of course — anything that needs strict brand control or longer-form narrative still has to go back to real shoots and editing. But for 80% of the everyday short-form assets, these 30 templates will get you most of the way there.

If you want to turn Gemini into your own video generation helper, take a look at Gemini Gems Tutorial: Easily Build Your Own AI Assistant — you can package this prompt structure into a dedicated Gem so you don’t have to paste the whole thing every time.

If you’d rather skip the work entirely, you can also check out Awesome Gemini Gems, where I’ve collected a bunch of Gems I’ve prepared. I’ll probably add some video-generation Gems in the future too — drop by anytime.

Hope these 30 templates are actually useful for your content work.

As with every other post, if you paste these in directly and find the output isn’t quite the same — AI generation is just random like that (lol).
entry.summary: After Google launched Gemini Omni at I/O 2026 in May, video generation in the Gemini App got a generational swap — it replaced Veo 3.1, with 10-second clips, native synced audio, and Chinese text rendering all coming together. This post first clears up how Omni in the Gemini App and Veo 3.1 on the developer side actually split the work, then walks through what makes Omni strong and how to write prompts that hold up, and ends with 30 scenario-specific prompt templates — IG Reels hooks, product demos, Logo Reveals, Cinematic B-roll, Before-After transitions, quote cards, and Lifestyle / travel / food cuts — all copy-paste ready.

entry.title: Is Claude Code Auto Memory Hogging Your Memory? A Cleanup & Diagnostics Guide
entry.content:
Note
Auto Memory writes your feedback into ~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/. It doesn’t use RAM, but it does eat Context Window and disk space. The good news is the memory files are usually just KB in size — the real reason ~/.claude/projects/ balloons to hundreds of MB or even GB is the conversation jsonl files sitting in the same folder. Claude Code uses cleanupPeriodDays (30 days by default) to auto-clean old session files, but if you just ran a massive session recently, waiting 30 days is a bit much. You can use find to fish out the big files instead.

Intro

A friend pinged me out of the blue the other day: “Hey Ray, my disk just lost 300–500 MB out of nowhere, and every time I launch Claude my Context drops way faster than usual. Any ideas?”

I had a pretty good guess. They’d been hammering Claude Code hard recently, so my first instinct was to check ~/.claude/projects/. Sure enough, that folder alone had eaten 300+ MB — and that’s tied to Claude Code’s Auto Memory mechanism. So this post is about that.

I also wrote a previous post — What Is Claude Code Auto Memory? A Complete Guide to the Auto Memory Feature — covering the basics and how Auto Memory works. This one picks up from there to talk about what Auto Memory actually “occupies,” what’s really eating your disk and Context Window, and how to deal with it.

What does Auto Memory actually occupy?

Quick answer first — Auto Memory occupies two things, and neither of them is RAM:

ResourceHow muchWhy
Context WindowFirst 200 lines or 25KB of MEMORY.mdAuto-loaded at the start of every conversation
Disk spaceUsually just KB-levelMemory content is written as Markdown files in your Home directory

Note
Heads up — when I say “memory” in this post I don’t mean RAM. I mean Context Window and disk space.

So how do you find this MEMORY.md? It’s just the index file inside your project’s memory/ folder, at ~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/MEMORY.md. It lists all your memory files (Markdown files), one entry per line. Claude uses that index to find and read the corresponding memory content.

Context Window: how does MEMORY.md eat tokens?

Auto Memory content lives as Markdown files on disk. Every time you start a new conversation, Claude Code auto-loads the content of MEMORY.md into the Context Window so Claude can reference those memories during the conversation.

But MEMORY.md doesn’t get loaded in its entirety — there’s a limit:

Auto Memory only loads the first 200 lines or first 25KB (whichever comes first) of MEMORY.md into the Context Window.

In other words, if your MEMORY.md is over 200 lines or 25KB, the part past that threshold doesn’t get loaded into Context at all. This is also why some people swear Claude was supposed to remember something — they clearly told it, Claude even confirmed it remembered — but it has no recollection later. It just never saw that memory entry.

Here’s what an actual MEMORY.md looks like:

Each line is just a one-sentence index pointing at the actual memory file. With this kind of setup, even if your memory grows to dozens of entries, only these index lines get loaded into Context Window at startup. The actual detail behind each entry only gets read when Claude needs it in the conversation.

To make it concrete, the feedback_uiux_skill_for_ui_work.md mentioned above expands into something like this:

You’ll notice the name, description, type, originSessionId fields — the frontmatter format looks a lot like a Skill’s SKILL.md. You can think of Auto Memory as another flavor of Skill: Claude uses this metadata to figure out what kind of memory this is, which session it came from, and what it contains. That’s why the description field is critical.

If you ever find Claude can’t seem to recall a specific memory you thought it had, it’s probably because that file’s description isn’t clear enough — Claude can’t tell what the entry is about, so it doesn’t read it.

How to slim down MEMORY.md

If your MEMORY.md is approaching 200 lines, here are a few ways to trim it:

  1. Split details into Topic files: Keep MEMORY.md as just the index — one line per entry with a link, and put the actual content in separate .md files. Claude reads them on demand.
  2. Delete outdated memories: Just tell Claude “forget the previous rule about X” in the conversation, or manually delete the corresponding file under ~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/.
  3. Edit manually: Memory files are regular Markdown — open them in your editor and clean up.

Note
Most of the time you’d ask the AI to organize this for you rather than hand-editing — the whole point of Auto Memory is letting the AI manage these memories. Doing it manually kind of defeats the purpose. That said, understanding how the structure and loading mechanism work is still useful when something goes wrong and you need to debug it.

Disk space: the real culprit isn’t Auto Memory

Now the disk side — this is where most people misunderstand Auto Memory.

Let’s first see how much Auto Memory itself actually takes up. Using one of my own projects as an example, the memory folder looks like this:

The entire memory/ folder adds up to 52KB. At this scale, you’ll basically never need to clean it.

Now what about the whole projects/ folder?

Surprised? 339 MB! So who’s actually taking up all that space?

The answer is conversation jsonl — Claude Code saves every single conversation as a .jsonl file, where each line is a JSON object containing your prompt, Claude’s response, tool calls, tool results — everything written down. For long conversations or sessions with heavy file reads/writes, a single file can easily hit MB-level.

See for yourself:

The biggest single conversation file is already up to 8 MB.

So next time you notice ~/.claude/projects/ growing, don’t blame Auto Memory — run du and you’ll see the actual culprit is these .jsonl files.

So how do I auto-clean these? Meet cleanupPeriodDays

OK, good news — there’s a way to handle this. Claude Code has a built-in auto-cleanup mechanism that deletes session files older than 30 days when it starts up. The setting is called cleanupPeriodDays.

Note
cleanupPeriodDays only cleans conversation logs — it doesn’t touch Auto Memory’s memory files. Whatever memories you’ve written stay put. No need to worry.

If you want to change the retention period, add this to ~/.claude/settings.json:

Now Claude Code will delete any jsonl older than 7 days on each startup. Minimum is 1 day; setting it to 0 throws an error.

If you don’t want to keep any conversation log at all (privacy reasons or just saving space), you can disable transcript writes entirely:

Stick that line in your ~/.zshrc or ~/.bashrc, and Claude Code will stop writing conversation jsonl from then on. The trade-off: you also lose the ability to claude --resume back to past conversations. Your call which one you’d rather have.

Cleaning out large files manually

cleanupPeriodDays handles the auto-cleanup, but if you just ran a huge session in the last couple weeks (like marathon subagent debugging), waiting 30 days is too long.

In that case, use find to track down the big files:

This finds all jsonl files larger than 50 MB under ~/.claude/projects/. Once you’ve checked you don’t need them, run -delete to remove them:

Note
Once you delete a session’s jsonl, that session can’t be brought back via claude --resume. If you still want to keep recent conversations, ls -lh first and check the modification times — only delete the ones you’re sure you won’t go back to.

While you’re at it, check which project folders are heaviest

Each folder under ~/.claude/projects/ corresponds to a directory you’ve opened with Claude Code. If a project is long since abandoned, there’s no point keeping its folder around. To see which folders are heaviest:

For any project you’re sure you won’t resume anymore, just delete the whole folder.

Anything else in that folder?

Besides conversation jsonl and memory/, each project folder may also contain a few UUID-named subfolders (no .jsonl extension — just folders). Those are scratch spaces used by subagents during task execution. You generally don’t need to touch them — cleanupPeriodDays will clean those up too.

If you’ve been running subagents with Worktree, the old worktrees also get auto-cleaned at startup, controlled by the same cleanupPeriodDays.

Hope this gave you a clearer picture of what Auto Memory actually “occupies.”

Next time your disk is running tight, du first to see what’s actually eating your space — don’t go blaming Auto Memory.
entry.summary: Opened `~/.claude/projects/` and noticed it keeps growing? The first instinct is usually to blame Auto Memory. Turns out Auto Memory doesn't eat your RAM at all — it actually eats Context Window (MEMORY.md loads only the first 200 lines or 25KB) and disk space. And the real culprit bloating that folder isn't memory files, it's conversation jsonl. This post walks through what Auto Memory actually consumes, the `cleanupPeriodDays` auto-cleanup mechanism, `CLAUDE_CODE_SKIP_PROMPT_HISTORY` for fully disabling transcript writes, and how to use `find` to clean out large files manually.

entry.title: What Are Claude Code Hooks? Auto-Run Lint, Block rm -rf, Inject Context on SessionStart — A Complete Guide
entry.content:
Note
Claude Code Hooks are “automatic trigger points” in Claude Code’s lifecycle — they fire your Shell command at the moment you specify. The common ones are PreToolUse (before a tool runs), PostToolUse (after a tool runs), UserPromptSubmit (every time you send a prompt), and SessionStart (when a session opens). You just write the config into the hooks block of settings.json and you’re set.

Intro

After using Claude Code for a while, you’ve probably hit a situation like this: every time it edits a file, you have to remind it to run Prettier and ESLint again (sure, you could just run npm run format yourself, but humans are lazy). Or you wanted to go fast so you flipped on --dangerously-skip-permissions, but now you’re nervous it might fire off something like rm -rf. Or your Context got Compacted and Claude completely forgot some important project conventions you’d already explained, so you have to explain them again — ugh.

So this post is about a really important Claude Code technique: Hooks. They let you “hook into” Claude Code’s lifecycle and trigger automated actions, making sure it does the right thing at the right moment.

I’ve also written Which Claude Code Files Should You Commit? CLAUDE.md, Settings, Rules, Skills — A Complete Breakdown which covers the layering concept of .claude/settings.json. This post is a follow-up that pulls the hooks feature buried in settings out into its own deep dive.

What’s the difference between Hooks, Skills, CLAUDE.md, and Rules?

Before getting into how to set up hooks, let’s quickly compare it against the other “teach Claude what to do” mechanisms, so it’s clearer what each one is actually for.

MechanismWho runs itWhen it triggersWhat it’s for
CLAUDE.mdClaudeLoaded on every conversationProject conventions, naming, workflows
RulesClaudeLoaded on startup OR when relevant paths are touchedModular rules that can fire by path
SkillClaudeClaude decides on its own when to useBundling an entire workflow into a reusable capability
HookYour ShellClaude Code lifecycle eventsForced automated actions

If the table didn’t quite click, think of it this way:

  1. CLAUDE.md and Rules are “rules”
  2. Skill is a “capability” — these three all rely on Claude reading them and choosing to follow
  3. Hook is different — hooks are run by Claude Code itself, Claude has no choice, the moment the trigger fires the command will run

Here’s a concrete example. Say in CLAUDE.md you write “run Prettier after every file edit”. Most of the time Claude listens, but occasionally it forgets (Context might’ve blown up?!). If you use a PostToolUse hook to set “run Prettier automatically after every Edit or Write“, then regardless of whether Claude feels like it, your Shell command is guaranteed to run.

So hooks fit the scenario of…

Things I absolutely cannot tolerate Claude missing

Like lint, format, security guardrails. The fuzzier stuff that needs Claude’s judgment is still better off in CLAUDE.md.

What does the hook config file look like?

All hook config goes into the hooks block of settings.json. The structure looks like this:

The outer key is the “event name” (like PreToolUse or PostToolUse), wrapping an array where each entry is a config group containing matcher (filters when this group activates) and hooks (the actual commands to run).

If you’ve read Claude Code Status Line Setup Guide (article in Mandarin), this structure should feel familiar — like Status Line, it’s “Claude Code hands data to your Shell, and you decide how to respond”.

Where does the config file go?

Like other Claude Code settings, hooks also have layers:

LocationScopeCommit it?
~/.claude/settings.jsonAll your projectsWon’t end up in the project repo
.claude/settings.jsonThe whole teamYes, commit it
.claude/settings.local.jsonJust you personallyDon’t commit (already in .gitignore)

In short: hooks the whole team should have (like lint) go in .claude/settings.json, personal preferences (like desktop notifications) go in ~/.claude/settings.json, and sensitive-path local rules go in .claude/settings.local.json.

What events are available?

Now we get to the main event of this post: “what events can we actually hook into?”

Honestly, Claude Code offers more than 20 events. Listing them all would scare people off, so I’ll group them by “how often I actually use them in real work”.

Here’s a diagram of a typical Claude Code session lifecycle, with the events that fire on each arrow — you can match it up with where each hook fits:

The most commonly used ones

EventWhen it firesClassic use case
PreToolUseBefore a tool runsBlock dangerous commands, rewrite parameters
PostToolUseAfter a tool succeedsAuto-format, run lint
UserPromptSubmitWhen you press Enter to send a promptInject extra context, filter sensitive info
SessionStartWhen a session opens or resumesLoad project context, output git status
StopWhen Claude finishes responding and is about to stopVerify the task was really completed
NotificationWhen Claude needs your attentionDesktop notifications, voice prompts

These six are the most frequently used — most real-world examples revolve around them.

Other ones that are rarer but worth knowing

EventWhen it firesClassic use case
PermissionRequestWhen a permission dialog is about to appearAuto-approve specific actions
PermissionDeniedWhen auto mode blocks a tool callLog blocked commands
PostToolUseFailureWhen a tool execution failsNotify on failure or auto-retry
SubagentStopWhen a subagent finishes a taskVerify subagent results
PreCompact / PostCompactBefore / after context compactionBack up the conversation, reinject rules after compaction
SessionEndWhen the session closesClean up temp files, write logs
ConfigChangeWhen a settings file is modified during the sessionAudit config changes
CwdChanged / FileChangedWhen the working directory or a watched file changesReload environment variables (pair with direnv)

At this point, you should see that hooks pretty much cover every node Claude Code passes through. Almost anything you can imagine in the shape of “I want X to run automatically when Y happens” is doable — the concept is the same as the “lifecycle events” you’d find in a framework.

How do you write a matcher?

Time to talk about how to actually write the hook condition.

You saw the hook example earlier looks like this:

matcher decides “under what conditions this hook group activates”, and what it compares against depends on the event. For the commonly used PreToolUse / PostToolUse, it compares against the “tool name”, so the most intuitive way to write it is to just drop the tool name into the matcher field.

Here’s a concrete example — this hook is “before Claude runs Bash, show a reminder in the terminal”:

After this, you’ll see the Bash is about to run reminder pop up:

The value of matcher is the tool name Bash, meaning “this hook only fires when Claude uses the Bash tool”.

Note
Why use jq here instead of echo? Because Claude Code talks to hooks in “JSON format”. If you just write echo "Bash is about to run", what Claude Code receives is unstructured plain text — it doesn’t know what you want it to do, so it just treats it as an execution log entry and Claude never sees it. But jq -n '{ systemMessage: "Bash is about to run" }' produces a JSON output, which is basically telling Claude Code “this field is called systemMessage, please show its contents to Claude”. When Claude Code sees that recognized format, it actually feeds the message in.

If you want to watch multiple tools at once, separate them with |. For example, this hook is “run Prettier automatically every time Claude uses Edit or Write to modify a file”:

The command looks a bit complicated, so let me break it down:

  1. When Claude finishes editing a file with Edit / Write, Claude Code sends a JSON payload to this hook that looks roughly like { "tool_input": {"file_path": "/path/to/file.ts" }, ... }, telling you which file it just changed.
  2. Then jq -r '.tool_input.file_path' parses that JSON and extracts the value of tool_input.file_path (the file path). The -r means “give me raw text, no quotes”.
  3. | (pipe) — think of it like a relay baton: “take the output of the previous command and feed it as input to the next one”.
  4. xargs takes that incoming text and “appends it as an argument to the next command”, so what actually runs is npx prettier --write /path/to/file.ts.
  5. npx prettier --write calls Prettier to format that file.

Note
prettier is a code formatter. The --write flag means “actually modify the file in place”. If you’d rather just see the diff without writing, swap it for --check. If your project uses ESLint instead of Prettier, replace npx prettier --write with npx eslint --fix.

Basically, in later examples, whenever you see jq -r '.xxx', it’s doing this same thing:

jq -r parses the JSON that Claude Code sent in, pulls out the field you want, and hands it off to the next command. The jq -n from earlier is the reverse — it “generates” a JSON payload from scratch to feed back to Claude Code.

Finally, if you want to “trigger no matter what tool runs”, just leave matcher as an empty string:

This hook appends the tool name to a log file before any tool is used by Claude — basically logging every tool call across the whole session.

This covers 95% of cases. If you want to look up what each tool is called, see my earlier post What do Claude Code’s built-in tools actually do? Understanding how it picks tools — it lists every tool by name.

Advanced: use regex to match MCP tools

The remaining 5% is when you want to match tools provided by an MCP server. MCP tools are named like mcp__<server>__<tool> — for example, the search tool from GitHub’s MCP server is called mcp__github__search_repositories.

If you want to watch every tool from a given MCP server at once, use the regex .* to match anything:

.* is regex for “any character”, so mcp__github__.* means “starts with mcp__github__, followed by anything”. Claude Code’s rule for when to use regex is simple: if the matcher is made of anything other than letters, digits, _, and |, it runs as a JavaScript regex; otherwise it’s compared as a literal string. So earlier writings like Bash or Edit|Write won’t accidentally be treated as regex.

Not all events match on tool name

Everything above was tool-related events, but some events don’t match on tool name — they match on some other “category of the event itself”.

The most common example is SessionStart, which matches on “how the session was started”. This hook is “when a session rebuilds after Context is Compacted, remind Claude of the project rules”:

When you see echo 'Reminder: use pnpm...', you might be thinking…

“Wait, didn’t you just say echo doesn’t work with PreToolUse? How come it works here?”

That’s because SessionStart and UserPromptSubmit are two special events — whatever the hook outputs to stdout gets automatically picked up by Claude Code as “extra context to inject for Claude”, so echo works and Claude sees it. Other events (like the PreToolUse we saw earlier) have to go through jq -n '{systemMessage: ...}' JSON format to talk to Claude Code.

matcher: "compact" restricts this to “only fire when the session rebuilds after Compact” — opening Claude Code for the first time or resuming an old session won’t trigger it. SessionStart‘s valid values are startup (first open), resume (resuming an old session), clear (cleared context), and compact (after compaction).

Notification follows similar logic — it matches on “notification type”. The common ones are permission_prompt (Claude is waiting for your approval) and idle_prompt (Claude has nothing to do and is waiting for the next message); rarer ones include auth_success, elicitation_dialog, elicitation_complete, and elicitation_response. For what each event’s matcher corresponds to, check the official hooks reference — I won’t list them all here.

Advanced: use if to filter on tool arguments too

matcher alone isn’t precise enough because it only compares tool names. If you want to say “only trigger the hook when Claude runs git push, ignore other Bash commands”, you need the more granular if field:

What this example does: when Claude wants to run any Bash command starting with git push, the hook outputs a blocking message and uses exit 2 to block the action. But if Claude wants to run git status or npm test, the hook won’t even fire.

The difference between the two filter fields:

  • matcher: "Bash" is the first-level coarse filter: “only look at the Bash tool”.
  • if: "Bash(git push *)" is the second-level fine filter — it also matches the actual command Bash is about to run. * is the “anything after this” wildcard, using the same syntax as the permissions settings.

The biggest upside is “the hook subprocess only spins up when the condition is actually matched”. Compared to firing the hook every time and then using jq to parse arguments to decide what to do, the performance is way better.

Note
The if field was added later, so older versions of Claude Code might not recognize it and just ignore it. If you wrote if but the hook behaves as if it isn’t there, run claude --version first to check your version and update.

How does a hook talk to Claude Code?

Now that matcher makes sense, let’s look at how a hook actually “exchanges messages” with Claude Code. Here’s the smallest possible example:

To give you a PreToolUse example: when Claude wants to run npm test, your hook receives a JSON payload like this on stdin:

Note
A lot of people writing their first hook trip up here: they assume exit 1 will block. But only exit 2 blocks — any other non-zero exit code doesn’t.

Using JSON output for finer control

Are exit 0 and exit 2 your only options? Not quite — you can do more granular things (like auto-approving, or directly rewriting the command Claude is about to run). The trick is exit 0 plus outputting a JSON payload to stdout:

permissionDecision has four values for PreToolUse:

  • allow: skip the permission prompt and allow directly
  • deny: block the action and feed the reason back to Claude
  • ask: fall back to the normal permission flow and prompt you
  • defer: the hook has no opinion, go back to the normal permission flow (same effect as not writing this field)

Note
Don’t mix exit 2 and JSON output. When Claude Code sees exit 2, it reads stderr as the reason and ignores any JSON on stdout. Pick one approach: either exit 2 with stderr, or exit 0 with JSON.

Five hook types

All the examples so far used type: "command", which is the most common form. But Claude Code has four others:

TypeHow it runsWhat it’s for
commandRun a Shell commandMost situations — flexible and simple
httpPOST event data to a URLHooking into a remote service for shared auditing or org-wide validation
mcp_toolCall a tool on a connected MCP serverReusing existing MCP tools as hook logic
promptHand data to a Claude model to decideSituations needing semantic judgment instead of hard-coded rules
agentDispatch a subagent with tools to verifySituations where you need to actually read files or run commands to decide

90% of people writing hooks stop at command, but the other three (especially prompt) are pretty worth knowing about — they upgrade what hooks can do from “dumb rules” to “gatekeepers with judgment”.

Here’s a prompt hook example. Say you want another model to check whether Claude really finished the requested task after it stops talking:

When this hook fires, Claude Code automatically calls a model to do a single-turn yes/no decision. If it replies that the task wasn’t finished, the main Claude keeps going — basically a free quality-control checkpoint.

Real-world examples

Enough concept, time for actual examples. The ones below are the ones I find most useful day-to-day, highest bang-for-buck.

Auto-format modified files

After Claude uses Edit or Write to modify a file, run Prettier automatically to fix the formatting:

This uses jq to parse the file_path out of the JSON stdin and pipe it to Prettier — all in one line. If your system doesn’t have jq, on Mac it’s brew install jq, on Ubuntu it’s apt install jq.

If your project uses ESLint instead of Prettier, swap npx prettier --write for npx eslint --fix.

This hook is tied to the project’s lint/format rules, so put it in .claude/settings.json and commit it for the whole team.

Block dangerous commands like rm -rf

It’s easier to maintain hooks as standalone Shell scripts. Start by creating .claude/hooks/block-rm.sh:

I’m using the POSIX character class [[:space:]] instead of \s here because \s is a GNU grep extension — macOS’s default BSD grep doesn’t necessarily support it, which would silently break the match. [[:space:]] is the POSIX standard syntax and works in every grep on Mac and Linux.

The (/([[:space:]]|$)) part forces / to be followed by whitespace or end-of-line, so:

  • rm -rf /: blocked
  • rm -r /: blocked
  • rm -rf /tmp/foo: allowed, because what follows / isn’t whitespace or end-of-line

This pattern is intentionally conservative — it only blocks literal rm -rf / and rm -r /. If you want to go harder (like blocking rm -rf /*, rm -rf ~, rm -rf $HOME and other variants), follow the same model and chain more grep -qE checks, each one with its own exit 2.

Don’t forget to make it executable:

Then register it in .claude/settings.json:

$CLAUDE_PROJECT_DIR is a built-in Claude Code environment variable — it expands to the project root, safer than hardcoding an absolute path.

Note
This hook fires even under --dangerously-skip-permissions. PreToolUse runs before the permission mode check, so hooks are one of the few mechanisms that can actually block actions in “bypass permissions” mode.

This kind of safety guardrail goes in .claude/settings.json committed to the repo so the whole team is protected. If you only want personal insurance, putting it in ~/.claude/settings.json is fine too.

Reinject project context after Compact

Once Context gets Compacted, Claude often forgets important conventions. You can use SessionStart with the compact matcher to inject a reminder every time after compaction:

What SessionStart and UserPromptSubmit hooks output to stdout gets added as extra context for Claude — that’s what makes these two events different from the others.

If you want to inject dynamic content (like the current git branch or uncommitted files), swap the echo for your own script:

This hook is best placed in .claude/settings.json, because “use pnpm” and “run pnpm test before commit” are internal project conventions — committing it means everyone who clones the repo picks it up automatically, no one has to set it up themselves.

Mac desktop notification

Get a notification when Claude is waiting for your permission or your next prompt, so you can confidently go do other things:

On Linux, swap osascript for notify-send; on Windows PowerShell, use MessageBox.Show — same idea.

Note
On Mac the first run might not show anything, because osascript sends notifications through the built-in Script Editor app. You need to go to System Settings → Notifications, find Script Editor, and enable its notification permission. If Script Editor isn’t even showing up in the list, run osascript -e 'display notification "test"' once in Terminal first — that will make it appear in the notification settings list.

Desktop notifications are purely personal preference — put this in ~/.claude/settings.json, no need to commit.

Send every Claude action to Discord

If you don’t want to babysit the terminal, or you want to share what Claude is up to with the team via a Discord channel, you can use a PreToolUse hook to call a Discord webhook.

First, generate a webhook URL in your Discord channel settings, then export it in your shell profile (don’t hardcode it in the script — committing it to a repo basically leaks it):

Next, create ~/.claude/hooks/discord-notify.js:

Finally, register it in ~/.claude/settings.json (using personal settings so it works across all projects):

matcher: "" means “notify on any tool”, so no matter what Claude is about to do — Read, Edit, Bash — you’ll get a Discord message. If you want different emoji, colors, or fields per tool type, extend along these lines.

This whole hook is for personal notifications and the webhook URL is yours alone — put it in ~/.claude/settings.json at the personal layer, no need to commit.

Note
The webhook URL should always be read from an environment variable, never hardcoded in the script. If you really want to share one webhook with the team, the hook itself can live in .claude/settings.json, but the webhook URL should still only live in each person’s shell profile or .claude/settings.local.json (which doesn’t get committed).

Log every Bash command

If you want to audit which Bash commands Claude has actually run, add a one-liner to PostToolUse:

Every Bash command Claude runs gets appended to ~/.claude/bash.log, easy to review later. This is personal auditing — put it in ~/.claude/settings.json.

How do multiple hooks combine?

When you set multiple hooks on the same event, they all run in parallel — they don’t block each other, and Claude Code combines all the results at the end. For PreToolUse, the permission decision follows the “strictest wins” rule: deny beats ask, ask beats allow.

A concrete example — you can set both “log every Bash command” and “block rm -rf” at the same time:

When Claude tries to run rm -rf /tmp/build, both hooks fire at the same time — the log gets written, the rm gets blocked. The log doesn’t miss the entry just because rm was blocked, because the two hooks run independently.

How do you confirm a hook actually ran?

You’ve set up a hook but aren’t sure if it’s even firing? Claude Code gives you two ways to check.

The first is the /hooks command — type /hooks inside Claude Code to bring up a list of currently configured hooks, grouped by event, each one drillable to see its matcher and command. /hooks is read-only — to actually edit a hook, you still need to edit settings.json.

The second is the debug log. When you launch, add --debug-file /tmp/claude.log:

Then open another window and run tail -f /tmp/claude.log — every hook trigger will show its full stdout, stderr, and exit code in the log. If you only want hook-related output without other debug noise drowning it out, use claude --debug hooks with a filter.

Further reading

  • Which Claude Code Files Should You Commit? CLAUDE.md, Settings, Rules, Skills — A Complete Breakdown: once you’ve nailed down the layering of .claude/settings.json, hooks make a lot more sense.
  • What do Claude Code’s built-in tools actually do? Understanding how it picks tools: to write a hook you first need to know the tool names that matcher targets.
  • Agent Skills: More Token-Efficient Than MCP: Skills and Hooks are complementary — Skills give capability, Hooks give guarantee.
entry.summary: When you're developing with Claude Code, do you find yourself constantly telling it to run Prettier again? Or worrying that one day it'll accidentally fire off rm -rf? This post walks through how Claude Code Hooks work — when events like PreToolUse, PostToolUse, UserPromptSubmit, SessionStart, Notification, and Stop fire, how to set up matcher and command in settings.json, how to block actions with exit code 2 — and runs through practical hooks: auto-format, blocking rm -rf, reinjecting project context after Compact, Mac desktop notifications, plus prompt / agent hook types and /hooks debugging.

entry.title: Can a Blog Really Make Tens of Thousands of Dollars a Month? Real Google AdSense Numbers, Explained
entry.content:
Intro

This post is about whether blogging actually makes money. I’ll start with my own blog’s three revenue sources (Google AdSense, Buy Me A Coffee, Portaly) at roughly an 8:1:1 split, then zoom in on Google AdSense — CPM vs. CPC, how AdBlock kills impression revenue, my Google Analytics monthly active users, the AdSense performance report (page views, impressions, clicks, CPC, page RPM), the actual monthly earnings math, and finally my own ad placement strategy.

Why I wanted to write this

Recently a few friends saw I’ve been running a blog and got curious about how much money it actually makes. So this post exists to break the “blogging is super lucrative” myth they were operating under. xD

It’s a pretty common misconception. The real numbers are nowhere near what people imagine, so I figured a dedicated post would help. The clickbait-y “tens of thousands a month is possible!” title is on purpose — it’s the first thing most people think when they hear “blog revenue” (right?).

Note
This post only covers Google AdSense as the main thread. Buy Me A Coffee and Portaly are small for an obvious reason — not every visitor donates — so I’m not expanding on those.

If you haven’t read my earlier post Why Writing Posts Can Earn Money — Google AdSense and Liker, Explained, that one’s a more entry-level take on how blog revenue works. This one’s the “five years later, actual results” version.

Breaking down my blog’s revenue

Quick list — my blog’s main revenue sources right now:

  • Google AdSense
  • Buy Me A Coffee
  • Portaly

Split roughly 8:1:1 — AdSense is the bulk, the other two are more “occasional reader appreciation” territory. So the rest of this post is mostly about AdSense, with the others getting maybe a line each.

How does AdSense calculate revenue?

The concept isn’t complicated — it boils down to whether an ad got seen, and whether the people who saw it clicked. Details get affected by ad type, placement, topic category, and so on, but the core logic is just those two things.

And both of those tie tightly to one thing: traffic. More traffic means more impressions, and more impressions mean more shots at clicks.

What’s my traffic actually like?

I pulled up Google Analytics for the last 30 days — about 18,000 active users.

Honestly that’s not a big number. Compared to sites pulling hundreds of thousands or millions a month, mine’s a drop in the bucket.

What are CPM and CPC?

Two key AdSense concepts: CPM and CPC.

AdSense pays via two main pricing models:

TermFull nameWhat it counts
CPMCost Per MilleRevenue per 1,000 impressions
CPCCost Per ClickRevenue per click

In short — CPM is “I earn something just by the ad being seen”, CPC is “I only earn when someone actually clicks”.

CPC unit pricing is usually much higher than CPM, and for a reasonable reason: a click means the user is actually interested and might convert into a purchase, so advertisers pay more. CPM revenue depends on placement, ad type, and — crucially — whether the user has AdBlock on.

If the user has AdBlock on, the ad request never even fires, so impressions and clicks both zero out. No CPM, no CPC. That’s why some sites pop up a message asking readers to whitelist them.

Personally I’m not into that vibe — it feels like guilt-tripping readers — so I just leave a quiet hint and call it a day.

It keeps the reading experience clean. And long-term that’s better for the blog’s growth anyway.

The real numbers: how much did I make this month?

OK enough concepts — let’s look at the actual AdSense “Performance” report. I’m pulling “last 28 days” alongside “the 28 days before that” so the trend is easier to read.

The report has these columns:

  • Page Views
  • Page RPM (revenue per 1,000 page views)
  • Impressions
  • Clicks
  • CPC (Cost Per Click)
  • Page CTR

My last 28 days roughly:

MetricValue
Page Views30,100
Page RPM$0.73
Impressions61,400
Clicks106
CPC$0.21
Page CTR0.35%

Honestly, $0.21 CPC is low. Some topics (finance, insurance, software subscriptions) can hit $1+ CPC. A $0.73 page RPM isn’t great either.

So how do those numbers add up into total revenue? Easiest way is impressions × page RPM / 1000 — because the page RPM AdSense reports is already the normalized average of all your CPC + CPM revenue per 1,000 impressions for that period. So reversing it gets you back to monthly revenue:

Plugging my numbers in:

About $44.82 a month (AdSense actually shows $44.84 — the 0.02 gap is because the 0.73 RPM has already been rounded in the report, so reversing the math loses a little precision).

Sounds OK? But this is the Google-reported number before tax and cross-border payment fees, so what actually lands is even less. And it’s not stable month to month — skip a few weeks of new posts or miss a search-trend topic and traffic drops, revenue drops with it. Here’s my Google Analytics trend from a period when I wasn’t taking the blog seriously:

Daily ad revenue back then was under $0.01.

Here’s the Google AdSense revenue snapshot at the time I was writing this post:

My ad placement strategy

Ad placement is a thing AdSense bloggers obsess over. My approach has always been “don’t hurt the read”, so I keep them in:

  • Top of article
  • Bottom of article
  • Article list (one ad inserted every five posts)
  • Sidebars (left and right)
  • Anchored slots (floating-ish, but pinned to top or bottom only)

Note
I usually browse my own blog with AdBlock on, because Google AdSense has strict rules against clicking your own ads. I got banned for two months once after accidentally clicking my own a few times. QQ…

If you were optimizing for “squeeze every drop of revenue”, my density would actually be on the low side. To really push it you could also add:

  • Mid-article ads (interrupting the read)
  • Overlay ads (covering part of the page)
  • Forced-countdown ads (can’t close until the timer ends)
  • Auto-playing video ads with sound

Those formats genuinely boost revenue. But reader experience hits the floor. I personally hate that stuff as a reader — I close the tab the moment an ad cuts into my read, no return visit, no RSS subscription, definitely no recommending it to a friend.

For more on ad placement, I wrote about this years ago in How to Increase Your Google AdSense Revenue (Placement Tips) — finer-grained placement breakdown over there if you want to go deeper.

So can you actually make tens of thousands a month?

Back to the clickbait question — can blogging hit tens of thousands of dollars a month? Possible, but the bar is brutal.

You’d probably need to hit all of these at once:

  • Traffic in the millions, not in the 18,000 range
  • Topics in the high-CPC niches (finance, insurance, SaaS, shopping)
  • Aggressive ad placement that somehow doesn’t get you AdBlocked
  • Multi-language operation (English traffic especially) to push CPM and CPC ceilings beyond a single country
  • Continuous content output and ongoing SEO maintenance

Miss any one of those and the number gets cut in half — or worse.

So for most independent bloggers, treating AdSense as a primary income source isn’t realistic. A more honest framing is “side income + long-term asset accumulation”.

Closing thoughts

I’m not trying to talk you out of starting a blog. Actually I’d argue the opposite — the long-term benefits of blogging go way beyond ad revenue. Building a personal brand, organizing what you know, training how you communicate — those payoffs dwarf ad earnings. I wrote about this in AI Is Everywhere — Why I Still Recommend Blogging (article in Mandarin) if you want to dig in.

But if you’re walking in motivated purely by “making money from blogging”, this post is here to calibrate expectations. Blogs do make money, but it’s nowhere near as juicy as you’d think — more like a piggy bank that slowly grows, not an ATM.

Hopefully this gave you a clearer picture of what blog revenue actually looks like.
entry.summary: This post is about whether blogging actually makes money. I start with my own blog's three revenue sources (Google AdSense, Buy Me A Coffee, Portaly) at roughly an 8:1:1 split, then zoom in on Google AdSense. Covers CPM vs. CPC, how AdBlock kills impression revenue, my Google Analytics monthly active users, the AdSense performance report (page views, impressions, clicks, CPC, page RPM), how to calculate actual monthly earnings, and finally my own ad placement strategy and why I try to balance revenue against reader experience.

entry.title: Claude Code Routines: How to Set Up /schedule (and Why It Pushes to claude/ Instead of main)
entry.content:
Note
Claude Code has three ways to schedule work: Routines (cloud-based, runs even with your computer off), Desktop scheduled tasks (local, app needs to be open), and /loop (session-level, terminal needs to stay open). The /schedule CLI is the entry point for Routines — minimum 1-hour interval, and by default it only pushes to claude/-prefixed branches. To push to main, flip “Allow unrestricted branch pushes” on the web side.

Intro

I’ve been using Claude Code’s /schedule for a while now. It builds a scheduled task that fires at whatever time I set, and after the run I usually have it push the result to GitHub. The thing is, I kept hitting this one situation — open GitHub the next day and the new branch is claude/main-xxxx instead of the main I asked for.

So this post is me writing up the scheduling feature in Claude Code, while also pinning down the claude/main-xxxx thing that’s been bugging me.

Oh, and if you’ve already read Claude Cowork Dispatch and Remote Control (article in Mandarin), think of this post as a parallel branch — Dispatch and Remote Control let you control Claude right now from your phone, while scheduling lets it work for you automatically while you’re not around.

Three ways to schedule in Claude Code

Before we get to /schedule, here’s a quick comparison of the three scheduling options Claude Code offers:

Routines (Cloud)Desktop scheduled tasks/loop
Where it runsAnthropic cloudYour computerYour computer
Computer needs to be on?NoYesYes
Session needs to be open?NoNoYes
Can see local files?No (fresh clone each run)YesYes
MCP / ConnectorsPer-routine configInherits local setupInherits session setup
Minimum interval1 hour1 minute1 minute

With that out of the way, the rest of this post focuses on Routines (Cloud).

What are Routines?

In short, a Routine is a prompt plus the repo it should work in (and a few other settings), parked on Anthropic’s cloud. It fires on whatever schedule or trigger you set up, and runs your task on its own.

Two main things separate it from a regular Claude Code session:

  1. It runs on Anthropic’s cloud. Not on your computer — so it still fires when you’re asleep, when you’ve gone out, or when your laptop’s off.
  2. It runs autonomously. No permission prompts (no Yes/No popups), it just follows your prompt all the way through. Which means the prompt has to be really clear — otherwise it might do something you didn’t expect.

Note
If you want to squeeze the most out of your monthly subscription, Routines are actually pretty useful for recurring tasks — checking for new PRs to review daily, summarizing the week’s commit log on Fridays, opening a monthly PR to update the stats chart in your README, that kind of thing.

Routines currently support three trigger types:

This post zooms in on /schedule.

Creating a schedule with /schedule

There are two ways to create a Routine:

  1. Web: claude.ai/code/routines
  2. CLI: /schedule

Either works — they both edit the same record, so creating or editing on one side syncs to the other instantly.

Basic usage:

Just type /schedule with nothing after it, and Claude walks you through it conversationally — asking what the prompt should be, which repo, how often it should run, etc.

If you already know exactly what you want, you can write it inline:

Or for a one-off reminder:

Claude parses the time you wrote, converts it to a concrete timestamp, confirms with you, and saves it.

Note
The /schedule CLI only creates Schedule-triggered Routines. For API or GitHub event triggers, you need to edit the routine on claude.ai/code/routines.

The CLI also has commands for managing existing routines:

CommandPurpose
/schedule listList all your routines
/schedule updateEdit a routine (including setting cron)
/schedule runTrigger right now, don’t wait for the next slot

Note
If you’re really lazy, you can also just tell the AI to use /schedule to set the schedule for you…

Why does it push to claude/xxx when I asked for main?

OK now the part that confused me — the “I asked for main but it pushed to claude/xxx“ thing.

When a Routine runs a prompt that pushes code, Claude can only push to claude/-prefixed branches by default. So even if your prompt says “push directly to main when done”, Claude opens something like claude/main or claude/update-main-xxxx and pushes there. Your actual main stays untouched.

This isn’t a bug — it’s a feature (really). The reasoning is to “stop routines from accidentally messing up important branches”. Scheduled tasks run autonomously, nobody’s watching, and there are no permission prompts. If they could write straight to main, one badly written prompt could blow up a production project.

So how do you handle it? Two options:

  1. Live with the default. Let it push to claude/xxx, then open a PR for review. This is the recommended workflow — since nobody’s watching when the routine runs, having changes land on a separate branch first and merging when you have time is just a lot safer.
  2. Turn on “Allow unrestricted branch pushes”. If you really want it to push to main directly, you can flip this in the Permissions section when editing the routine. No more branch prefix restriction — but you’d better make sure the prompt is rock-solid and won’t do anything you don’t want.

If you really do want it to push straight to a specific branch, here’s how:

  1. Go to claude.ai/code/routines and click into the routine
  2. Hit the pencil icon to enter Edit routine
  3. Find the Permissions section at the bottom
  4. Check “Allow unrestricted branch pushes“ for that repo
  5. Save

Note
If your main is protected with a branch protection rule, GitHub will still block the push even with “Allow unrestricted branch pushes” turned on. At that point you either add an exception to the protection rule, or just go through the PR flow — which is probably more practical anyway.

Where it runs and what it can access

Besides the wrong-branch thing, there are a couple of Routine details that are easy to miss the first time you set one up.

Every run is a fresh clone of the default branch
