Every time a Routine fires, it clones a fresh copy of the default branch from your chosen repo — it doesn’t carry over a working tree from the last run. So you can’t write a schedule that “continues yesterday’s half-finished work”. Every run starts from scratch.

If your task needs to reference past state, write that state into a file in the repo, or hook up to a third-party service.

One-off schedules don’t count against the daily cap

Routines have a daily run cap (the max number of runs per day), but one-off schedules don’t count against that cap — they just draw from your subscription’s general usage. So if you want to use scheduling as a “remind me in two weeks” tool, you can stack a bunch of these without burning through the daily cap.

Managing routines

Two ways to manage existing routines.

On the CLI side there’s /schedule list, /schedule update, and /schedule run for listing, editing, and force-triggering.

On the web side, go to claude.ai/code/routines, click into a routine’s detail page, and you get more:

  • Run now — fire it immediately without waiting for the next slot
  • Repeats toggle — pause or resume the schedule; pausing doesn’t delete the routine itself
  • Edit — name, prompt, repo, environment, Connectors, trigger type
  • Delete — removes the routine; past run sessions stay in your session list

Speaking of which — every time a routine runs, a new session shows up in your session list. You can click in, see what Claude did, review changes, open a PR, or just keep the conversation going from where the routine left off.

Wrapping up

Claude Code’s scheduling feature isn’t all that complicated once you get it — the main things are: know the differences between the three scheduling types, learn how /schedule works, and remember the claude/-prefixed branch default. After that it’s just tuning trigger types and environment settings to match your needs.

My own habit at this point — daily “run this small thing every day” goes into Routines as a daily schedule, batch tasks that need local file access become Desktop scheduled tasks, and anything I want to poll repeatedly inside a current session uses /loop. They each have their own slot.

As for the “always pushes to claude/xxx“ thing — once I knew it was intentional design, I made my peace with it. Letting it push to claude/ and reviewing through a PR is honestly more reassuring anyway. If you’ve been hitting the same confusion, hopefully this saved you a trip into the docs.

Further reading

If you want to go broader with Claude Code, check these out next:

  • Claude Cowork Dispatch and Remote Control (article in Mandarin) — Another option for not having to sit at your computer, but this one’s about controlling Claude live from your phone, not automatic scheduling.
  • Want to develop with Claude Code? This guide takes you from beginner to advanced — A full Claude Code guide from basic usage to advanced development techniques.
  • What Is Claude Code Auto Memory? A Complete Guide to the Auto Memory Feature — Pairs nicely with scheduling — Auto Memory keeps your preferences across every scheduled run.
entry.summary: I scheduled a task with Claude Code's /schedule, came back to the repo next day, and asked myself: I told it to push to main, why are there a bunch of claude/xxx branches instead? This post walks through Claude Code's three scheduling options (Routines, Desktop scheduled tasks, /loop), how /schedule works on the CLI, Routines' three trigger types (Schedule, API, GitHub event), custom cron rules, the 1-hour minimum interval — and most importantly, why pushes land on claude/-prefixed branches and how to flip "Allow unrestricted branch pushes" to fix that confusing behavior.

entry.title: Why CSS overflow: scroll Shows No Scrollbar on macOS Chrome (vs. Windows)
entry.content:
Intro

Anyone who writes CSS has hit this — you add overflow: scroll to an element, open Chrome, no scrollbar. Send it to a coworker and there’s a gray scrollbar sitting on theirs. Same Chrome — what gives? This post starts from the overflow property itself.

A quick refresher on overflow

Before getting into the weird behavior, let’s revisit what overflow actually does.

In short, overflow decides how an element handles content that exceeds its own width/height — pretty basic CSS, but it’s used everywhere.

For example, imagine a div that’s 200px wide and 100px tall, but you stuff a long line of text or an image wider than 200px into it. Three things could happen visually:

  • The content just spills out of the container
  • The content gets clipped — anything past the bounds is gone
  • A scrollbar shows up and lets the user scroll through it

Which one happens depends on overflow.

The four common overflow values

Here’s a quick table:

ValueBehavior
visibleDefault. Content spills out past the container — nothing’s done about it.
hiddenContent past the bounds is clipped — invisible and unscrollable.
scrollAlways reserves space for a scrollbar, whether or not content overflows.
autoScrollbar appears only when content actually overflows.

The pair worth calling out is scroll vs. auto — they often get confused, and the difference is small. scroll is “scrollbar is always there”, auto is “scrollbar only when needed”.

So why doesn’t overflow: scroll show a scrollbar on my machine?

Because macOS defaults to “Overlay Scrollbars” — they only appear while you’re actively scrolling, otherwise they’re hidden, and they don’t take up layout space. Windows uses “Classic Scrollbars” — reserved width, always visible.

Two people opening the same page in the same Chrome version see different visuals — not because Chrome differs, but because the OS underneath does.

macOS’s “Overlay Scrollbars”

On macOS, scrollbar display is controlled by a system setting — System Settings → Appearance → Show scroll bars. Three options:

The default is “Automatically based on mouse or trackpad” — when you’re using a trackpad (or the built-in Mac trackpad), it follows the “While scrolling” behavior. Kind of a tongue-twister — in plain English: the scrollbar is invisible most of the time, a translucent thin bar fades in while you actively scroll, then fades out a few seconds after.

Chrome, Safari, and most Mac browsers follow this system setting by default. So even if you write overflow: scroll, the browser still uses Overlay Scrollbar rendering — no permanently visible scrollbar.

How do you confirm this is what’s happening? Two steps:

  1. Set macOS’s “Show scroll bars” to “Always”
  2. Reload (or reopen) the browser

Now the scrollbar shows up and stays visible. That confirms your CSS is fine — the OS setting was controlling what you see.

Why doesn’t Windows have this problem?

Because Windows has always used Classic Scrollbars — they take up a fixed strip (around 15–17px) on the right side, always visible, never fading.

So when your coworker opens the same page, the thin gray bar sits there on the right and overflow: scroll looks exactly like the CSS implies. No “where did the scrollbar go?” mystery.

Side note worth knowing — if you develop on Mac but your users are mostly on Windows, the content width you see is actually wider than what your users get (because their scrollbar steals some width). When doing RWD layout work, borrow a coworker’s Windows machine and check the layout there, otherwise you might ship something that feels cramped on the right.

How do I make both sides look the same?

If you want every user — regardless of OS — to see a scrollbar (e.g., so Mac users actually realize there’s more content to scroll), here are some options.

Option 1: Force it with ::-webkit-scrollbar

The most direct approach is the ::-webkit-scrollbar CSS pseudo-element. Give it an explicit width or height and Chrome switches that element’s scrollbar from Overlay to Classic — always visible.

After that, Mac Chrome finally shows a permanent scrollbar — and since it’s now a Classic Scrollbar, it eats some content width, making the layout closer to what Windows users see.

Two gotchas:

  • ::-webkit-scrollbar is a non-standard Webkit / Blink property. Chrome, Edge, Safari, and Opera support it; Firefox doesn’t.
  • iOS Safari’s support for ::-webkit-scrollbar is also limited — most of the time it still uses the system’s Overlay Scrollbar.

Option 2: Use standardized scrollbar-width and scrollbar-color

If you’d rather not use a non-standard property, CSS later standardized scrollbar styling with two W3C properties (CSS Scrollbars Module Level 1): scrollbar-width and scrollbar-color. The big difference from ::-webkit-scrollbar is that these are spec-backed.

  • scrollbar-width accepts auto, thin, or none — corresponding to default thickness, thin, or hidden.
  • scrollbar-color takes two color values — first one is the thumb, second is the track.

Browser support to watch out for: Firefox has supported these since 64. Chromium (Chrome, Edge) only got support from Chrome 121 onward. Safari needed Safari 26.2 for full support. If you still need to support older browsers, keep ::-webkit-scrollbar as a fallback.

Note
MDN notes that when scrollbar-width or scrollbar-color is set to a non-auto value, it overrides ::-webkit-scrollbar styles. So if you write both, mind the cascade.

Why not scrollbar-gutter?

You might be wondering — doesn’t CSS have scrollbar-gutter to reserve scrollbar space? Wouldn’t that solve this more cleanly?

Unfortunately no — it doesn’t apply to macOS’s Overlay Scrollbars.

scrollbar-gutter: stable was designed to reserve the layout space a scrollbar would need, to stop content from jumping when the scrollbar appears or disappears. But MDN explicitly says: when the browser is using overlay scrollbars, scrollbar-gutter has no effect.

In short — it solves the “content jumping” problem under Classic Scrollbars, where the bar takes up space. On macOS’s default Overlay Scrollbars, there’s no space being taken in the first place, so there’s nothing to reserve.

So if your goal is “make Mac Chrome always show a scrollbar”, scrollbar-gutter won’t get you there — go back to ::-webkit-scrollbar or scrollbar-width.

Closing thoughts

Back to the “they see it, I don’t” scenario from the top — the root cause isn’t bad CSS, and it isn’t a Chrome bug. It’s an OS-level scrollbar mechanism difference. macOS defaults to Overlay, Windows defaults to Classic, so overflow: scroll renders visually differently on each.

Now that you know, the next time you hit this you don’t have to question your CSS — it’s the OS — and you can reach for ::-webkit-scrollbar or the newer scrollbar-width to keep visuals consistent.

If you’re curious about how a browser actually turns CSS into pixels, check out The Browser Render Pipeline Every Frontend Engineer Should Know — it walks through the render process from the DOM tree all the way to compositing, and pairs well with this post for a fuller “why does the browser do that?” mental model.
entry.summary: You set overflow: scroll, then realize Mac Chrome shows no scrollbar — but your coworker on Windows sees one fine. This post starts with the four overflow values, then explains macOS's Overlay Scrollbar mechanism — why Chrome on macOS doesn't always show a scrollbar even with overflow: scroll. Three fixes: ::-webkit-scrollbar to force it, the newer standard scrollbar-width and scrollbar-color, and flipping macOS's "Show scroll bars" system setting to "Always".

entry.title: VSCode Opens Every Link in the Integrated Browser? How to Disable Auto Takeover
entry.content:
Intro

VSCode 1.109 shipped a new Integrated Browser, and once workbench.browser.openLocalhostLinks is on, every localhost link in the terminal or chat view gets pulled into the editor — tiny pane, painful to use. This post covers how to disable the two key settings (workbench.browser.openLocalhostLinks and simpleBrowser.useIntegratedBrowser), and how to still call the integrated browser manually from the command palette when you actually want it.

Why did this start happening?

This behavior isn’t something VSCode has always had — it landed in 1.109 (around January 2026) along with a new feature called the Integrated Browser. When it’s on, any localhost link VSCode detects gets pulled into the editor instead of being handed off to your default browser. The idea is you don’t have to switch apps to see the page.

The intent is fine, but in practice it’s pretty annoying. The editor is already split between the file explorer, the editor itself, output panels, and so on — adding another browser pane shrinks usable space further. Trying to test RWD or open DevTools in there is rough. QQ…

What’s the relationship between the Integrated Browser and Simple Browser?

If you’ve been using VSCode for a while you’ve probably heard of Simple Browser — an old built-in browser extension. It’s pretty limited:

  • Only shows static pages
  • Can’t log into auth-required sites
  • Doesn’t keep cookies

So most people only ever used it for a quick “is localhost up?” peek.

So what’s the relationship between the new Integrated Browser and Simple Browser?

In short, the Integrated Browser is an upgraded version of Simple Browser, but they coexist — the new one doesn’t replace the old. A setting decides which one the command palette opens.

The Integrated Browser fills in what Simple Browser was missing:

  • Log into auth-required sites (cookies and sessions persist)
  • Browse any page, not just localhost
  • Built-in DevTools — inspect elements directly
  • Send the selected element to Copilot as context

Basically VSCode stuffed a small Chrome-like browser into the editor, so you can fully view a page without leaving VSCode — instead of the half-broken view Simple Browser used to give.

So why does clicking a link auto-route there?

The Integrated Browser by itself is just “one more browser you can use”. The “link gets hijacked” part comes from a separate setting — workbench.browser.openLocalhostLinks. When that’s on, anywhere in VSCode (terminal, chat, Markdown preview, output panel) — clicking a localhost link routes to the Integrated Browser instead of your system default browser.

Note
The 1.109 release notes describe it as “you can turn this on”, implying it’s off by default. But in some setups it gets flipped on without you noticing — maybe you clicked through a prompt, or a plugin enabled it for you. Next thing you know, every localhost link is opening inside VSCode.

How to fix it

Now that you know which setting is the culprit, turn it off. Two ways — pick whichever you prefer.

Method 1: Through the Settings UI

Open VSCode Settings:

  • macOS: Cmd + ,
  • Windows / Linux: Ctrl + ,

In the search bar at the top, type:

You should see the setting — uncheck it. No restart needed, the change is immediate.

Method 2: Edit settings.json directly

If you’d rather edit JSON (or you sync settings across machines), this route works too.

Press Cmd + Shift + P (Ctrl + Shift + P on Windows) to open the command palette, then type:

That opens settings.json — add this:

Save it, and the next localhost link you click in the terminal will go back to your system browser.

While you’re here: Simple Browser’s own setting

After disabling workbench.browser.openLocalhostLinks, localhost links should behave normally again. But it’s worth knowing about another related setting — simpleBrowser.useIntegratedBrowser. This one belongs to the Simple Browser extension (a VSCode built-in extension), not to VSCode core. You can see it from the naming.

SettingBelongs toWhat it controls
workbench.browser.openLocalhostLinksVSCode coreWhether clicking a localhost link auto-routes to the integrated browser
simpleBrowser.useIntegratedBrowserSimple Browser (built-in extension)Whether running the Simple Browser command uses the new integrated browser

The prefix tells you where the setting comes from — workbench.* is VSCode’s own, simpleBrowser.* is the Simple Browser extension’s. So if you’ve disabled the core setting but the command palette still routes you to the new integrated browser, this one’s still on — search and disable.

What if I still want the integrated browser sometimes?

Disabling auto-takeover doesn’t make the integrated browser unavailable — you can still summon it when you want to.

Open the command palette (Cmd + Shift + P on macOS, Ctrl + Shift + P on Windows / Linux), then type:

Hit enter, paste in a URL, and it opens in an editor tab. This way you get it when you need it, but link clicks aren’t hijacked when you don’t.

Closing thoughts

The Integrated Browser as a feature is actually nice — it’s just the default auto-takeover that gets in the way for anyone using an external browser as their main dev browser. That tiny pane really isn’t a substitute for Chrome or Edge in daily work, so disabling the auto-route is the better default.

Hopefully this clears up VSCode’s Integrated Browser behavior and how to keep it from grabbing your links. If you want to keep going with your Mac dev environment cleanup, check out Installing Software on Mac: Homebrew vs. Direct Download vs. curl, Compared — pairs well with this one as part of a broader dev environment setup pass. See you in the next post.
entry.summary: VSCode 1.109 shipped a new Integrated Browser, and once workbench.browser.openLocalhostLinks is on, every localhost link in the terminal or chat view gets pulled into the editor — tiny pane, painful to use. This post covers how to disable the two key settings (workbench.browser.openLocalhostLinks and simpleBrowser.useIntegratedBrowser), and how to still call the integrated browser manually from the command palette when you actually want it.

entry.title: Vibe Coding's 2026 Upgrade: A Six-Stage Agentic Engineering SOP with Templates and a Real Case
entry.content:
Intro

If you’ve used Claude Code, Cursor, Codex, or any of these AI coding tools, you’ve probably hit the same wall: it feels magical when you start, but once you try to build a real project at scale, the gremlins show up — context overflows and AI forgets everything, you fix A and break B, AI overwrites good code while editing, multiple files get touched at once and you can’t roll any of it back cleanly.

None of these are tool problems. They’re workflow problems — you haven’t built a discipline for collaborating with AI.

Worth mentioning here: Andrej Karpathy coined the term “Vibe Coding” in February 2025 (original tweet). Then in April 2026, at Sequoia Ascent 2026, he upgraded the concept himself — the talk is titled “Software 3.0, Agentic Engineering, and Jagged Intelligence”, and the key line is:

“Vibe coding raises the floor. Agentic engineering is about extrapolating the ceiling.”

Translated into plain English: vibe coding raises the floor, so people who’ve never written code can still build things. Agentic engineering raises the ceiling — it’s how professional engineers should actually work.

So what’s the difference between an agentic engineer and a vibe coder? Based on the talk’s core message, the work isn’t about describing a need, letting AI write it, and shipping. Instead, AI plays the role of implementer and you play supervisor — designing specs, supervising plans, reviewing diffs, writing tests, building evaluation loops, managing permissions, isolating worktrees, maintaining quality. An agentic engineer doesn’t blindly accept AI-generated code — they wrap engineering discipline around AI speed.

I’ve previously written AI Vibe Coding: A Look at Today’s Mainstream BDD and SDD and Want to Start Vibe Coding? Fundamentals Beginners Should Pick Up First — the first one’s more methodology, the second’s more beginner basics. But both still sit inside the vibe coding frame. This post pushes that one level up, using agentic engineering discipline to lay out the full hands-on workflow. Every section ships with templates you can lift directly, and at the end we’ll walk a personal e-commerce MVP (Nuxt 3 + Supabase + Stripe) through the whole thing.

Note
Why insist on discipline? Per CodeRabbit’s December 2025 “State of AI vs Human Code Generation” report (analyzing 470 GitHub open-source PRs — 320 AI co-authored + 150 human-only), AI-written code has 1.7x more major bugs than human-written code and 1.57x more security issues overall. Within that, XSS vulnerabilities are 2.74x and insecure password handling 1.88x. AI generates fast but quality isn’t guaranteed — what makes it safe is the workflow you design to catch its mistakes.

Vibe Coding vs Agentic Engineering: Which one for 2026?

Before getting into the workflow, here’s a table laying out the differences:

DimensionVibe CodingAgentic Engineering
When introducedFeb 2025, KarpathyApr 2026, Karpathy’s upgrade
Core spiritDescribe by gut feel, AI writes, minimal reviewDesign spec, supervise plan, check diffs, write tests, build evals
When to useDemos, PoCs, throwaway projectsProduction services, long-term maintainable projects
Workflow disciplineLoose, improvisedStructured, has an SOP
Human roleA user describing ideasAn engineer supervising AI employees
Failure modeWorks, but can blow up anytimeSlower but more controlled

You can see from the table — these aren’t replacements for each other, they’re for different situations. Building a weekend toy? Vibe coding is fine. Building something other people will use, something you need to maintain? Time to switch to agentic engineering.

The SOP that follows is written entirely from the agentic engineering discipline.

The full workflow at a glance: one feature, requirement to ship

Before getting into details, here’s a flowchart that compresses the whole SOP into a feature lifecycle. Three roles — you (the user), Leader (main Claude), and the employees (Sub-agents + Skills). Seven steps total:

Key things to notice:

  • You only step in for steps ①, ⑥, and ⑦ (submit the requirement, read the report, review diffs + commit). Everything between ② and ⑤ is the Leader orchestrating.
  • Ideally Leader should act more like an orchestrator — break down, delegate, collect reports, push implementation to employees. In practice, the main agent often still picks up code itself, so unless you explicitly design the delegation flow (e.g. prompt directly with “please invoke X sub-agent”), don’t assume it auto-routes.
  • The ⚠ step is Anthropic’s officially recommended interview pattern — when a requirement is fuzzy, Leader uses AskUserQuestion to ask back, so downstream doesn’t go the wrong way.
  • ④ Code Review needs a fresh context — done by another Claude session or sub-agent to avoid self-confirmation bias.
  • ⑤ Acceptance tests are based on the spec (CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md), not whatever Leader made up.

Each step maps to a later section: ① ② in 1. Planning phase, ③ in 2. Division of labor + 3. Development discipline, ④ ⑤ in 4. Wrap-up validation, ⑦ in the commit cadence under 3. Development discipline.

If Leader goes off the rails mid-way, jump to the rollback table in 5. Mistake recovery. After you catch a trap, write it into 6. Memory system to make it a long-term rule.

1. Planning phase: Spec → Plan → Tasks, three-layer documentation

The first principle of agentic engineering is the plan should be documented. Mainstream frameworks line up here — GitHub’s 2025 Spec Kit (Specify → Plan → Tasks → Implement, each stage producing spec.md / plan.md / tasks.md files), Anthropic’s Claude Code best practices (Explore → Plan → Implement → Commit, with plan mode letting you press Ctrl+G to open the plan in your editor directly). The core idea is identical — plans and specs should be readable, editable files, not things that only live in a chat.

Specifically, three layers:

  1. Spec: what this project is, who it’s for, where the boundaries are
  2. Plan: which features to build, by when
  3. Tasks: how the current task breaks down, where you are right now

Each layer in its own file. Each solves a different context problem.

1.1 Spec: write a CLAUDE.md (with AGENTS.md) as your project constitution

Before you start, one important thing: different AI coding tools read different config files.

  • Claude Code only reads CLAUDE.md, not AGENTS.md (Anthropic’s official docs explicitly say “Claude Code reads CLAUDE.md, not AGENTS.md“)
  • Other tools (Cursor, Codex, Windsurf, Aider, etc.) read AGENTS.md

What is AGENTS.md?

AGENTS.md is an open standard OpenAI launched in August 2025, jointly developed with Amp, Google Jules, Cursor, Factory, and other AI tool vendors. In December 2025 OpenAI donated it to the Linux Foundation’s Agentic AI Foundation (AAIF), joining MCP (donated by Anthropic) and Goose (donated by Block) as the three founding projects of AAIF. As of May 2026, over 60,000 open-source projects use it, with support in 20+ AI coding tools — Amp, Codex, Cursor, Devin, Factory, Gemini CLI, GitHub Copilot, Jules, VS Code, and more.

How to set it up?

The most practical approach is both files, with no content duplication:

  • AGENTS.md holds the project conventions themselves (shared across tools)
  • CLAUDE.md starts with @AGENTS.md to import AGENTS content, then adds Claude-Code-specific rules + @ imports for ROADMAP / tasks

Or even simpler: ln -s AGENTS.md CLAUDE.md creates a symlink so one file serves both names (on Windows you need Administrator or Developer Mode — if neither is set, @AGENTS.md import is safer).

If you only use Claude Code and don’t care about cross-tool, just write CLAUDE.md. No need for AGENTS.md.

CLAUDE.md template

Copy this into your project’s root CLAUDE.md:

Note
Line 3 (@AGENTS.md) in the template above is for the dual-file setup. If you only have CLAUDE.md without AGENTS.md, delete that line (or Claude Code will complain it can’t find AGENTS.md).
Conversely, if you do go with the dual-file setup: AGENTS.md content looks the same as the CLAUDE.md template above, except no @AGENTS.md line (since it’s the import target itself). And the ## Read before starting section is better kept only in CLAUDE.md, because @import is Anthropic’s CLAUDE.md extension — other tools’ AGENTS.md implementations may not support it.

@import mechanism (this is the Claude Code key)

Notice the @<path> syntax in the ## Read before starting section of the template above — this is Anthropic’s CLAUDE.md extension (confirmed in official docs). When the AI starts up and hits @<path>, it pulls that file’s content into context too. It recurses up to 5 hops deep, and supports both relative and absolute paths (e.g. @~/.claude/personal-rules.md).

This mechanism matters because Claude Code only auto-loads CLAUDE.md itself (plus CLAUDE.md from ancestor paths) on startup — anything under docs/ like ROADMAP.md or tasks.md doesn’t get auto-read. If you write ROADMAP without @-referencing it from CLAUDE.md, the AI starting fresh in a new session can’t see your plan at all. You’d be writing for nothing.

Note
@<path> is Anthropic’s CLAUDE.md extension. The AGENTS.md spec itself doesn’t define an import syntax, so other tools’ AGENTS.md implementations may not support @import. So in cross-tool scenarios, it’s safer to inline important rules directly into AGENTS.md — don’t assume other tools will resolve @import. Claude Code still works as usual: pull the full AGENTS.md content via @AGENTS.md, then hook in plans via @docs/ROADMAP.md.

1.2 Plan: write a ROADMAP.md listing features and stages

CLAUDE.md (with AGENTS.md) is the project constitution — doesn’t change much. ROADMAP.md is the plan for this sprint or this month’s features — gets updated as development progresses.

ROADMAP.md template

Put it in docs/ROADMAP.md:

The ## Explicitly out of scope section matters. AI loves making up features and crossing boundaries — writing out what not to do helps keep it in lane.

1.3 Tasks: write a tasks.md to sync current progress

The third layer is current-task progress tracking. AI tools have built-in todo lists (Claude Code’s todo system, for example), but those live in context — once you run /compact, close the session and reopen, or context overflows, the list disappears with it. The more durable approach is to also have the AI write progress to an actual file.

tasks.md template

Put it in docs/tasks.md:

Before starting, just tell the AI: list the todos, sync them to docs/tasks.md, update the file every time you finish an item. That way even if you reopen a fresh session tomorrow, AI picks up the project by reading the file.

2. Division of labor: your three types of AI employees

Karpathy emphasized the agentic in agentic engineering for a reason — in 2026, you’re no longer the engineer writing code by hand. You’re the supervisor managing a team of AI employees. Claude Code’s 2025-2026 evolution gave us three employee configurations, each solving a different problem.

2.1 Skill: standard workflow injected into the main agent

A Skill is a markdown instruction pack stored at .claude/skills/<name>/SKILL.md. When Claude detects a task matching the Skill’s description, it auto-loads it into the main context. Skills share context and share the model with the main agent — good for repeatable standard moves.

Skill template (API conventions)

.claude/skills/api-conventions/SKILL.md:

Note
If you want the full Skill mechanism, see my previous post: Agent Skills: More Token-Efficient Than MCP — Demoed With Claude Code.

2.2 Sub-agent: dedicated employee with isolated context and isolated permissions

The biggest difference between a Sub-agent and a Skill: a sub-agent has its own context window, its own system prompt, its own tool permissions, and can even run a different model. After the main Claude agent delegates a task to a sub-agent, the sub-agent works in its own context and returns only a summary — the main agent’s context stays clean.

Files go in .claude/agents/<name>.md:

Sub-agent template (security-reviewer)

.claude/agents/security-reviewer.md:

Sub-agent template (test-writer)

.claude/agents/test-writer.md:

Sub-agent template (ui-engineer)

.claude/agents/ui-engineer.md:

When to pick Skill vs Sub-agent?

SituationPick SkillPick Sub-agent
Repeatable standard moves (API conventions, commit rules)✓
Need isolated context to avoid polluting main chat (deep search, code review)✓
Want to run a different model for cost savings (main Sonnet + sub Haiku)✓
Want to restrict tool permissions (reviewer reads but doesn’t write)✓
Want AI to auto-detect and use it, no manual trigger✓
Multi-step workflows with decision points✓

2.3 Agent Teams: advanced mode with parallel sessions

Agent Teams is a 2026 advanced mode — multiple Claude instances running in isolated worktrees, coordinating via git. Each agent claims its own task, merges its own work, resolves its own conflicts. Practical experience puts the sweet spot at 3-5 parallel agents — above 10, costs go up but the marginal benefit drops.

This mode is too complex for beginners. Master Skill + Sub-agent first, then consider Agent Teams when one person + one main Claude can’t keep up with the project.

3. Development discipline: three rules to stick to every day

Once you’re in implementation, three rules to enforce every day. Each one is a net catching a specific kind of AI mistake.

3.1 Add tests in the same turn as the code change

This rule comes up the most when reminding AI: every time you change code, add the tests in the same turn — don’t punt to “I’ll batch them up in a separate task later”.

Why insist? Because AI development has this very easy trap: fixing A and breaking B. You tell it to adjust some composable’s logic, the code that comes back looks fine on the surface, but it’s actually side-effected somewhere else and your eye can’t catch it. Tests are the net that catches that side effect.

So why insist on the same turn? Because tests added later tend to become tests that exist just to make code pass — not tests that actually verify behavior. When you write them in the same turn, the AI’s context for the implementation and the tests is connected — the requirement, the logic, and the cases all stay alive. If you punt to a later task, the context has drifted by then, and the cases AI writes will only catch the surface — not the real edge cases.

Test-along prompt template

Before starting / before each new feature, tell the AI:

3.2 UI changes always go to a dedicated Skill or Sub-agent

Any time the change involves UI/UX (layout, component styling, Tailwind class adjustments), call the dedicated UI employee — don’t let the main agent freestyle it.

Why split it?

Specialization, basically. The main agent is strong at architecture decisions and writing logic. But once UI/UX comes in, it tends to commit the usual sins: slapping !important on everything to override existing styles, color choices that show zero design system awareness, spacing like mt-5 mb-3 with no logic.

UI-dedicated employees usually have design system knowledge, color conventions, and accessibility principles baked in. They know filled inputs don’t need borders, focus rings go through tokens, touch targets are at least 44px — all the basics.

UI delegation prompt template

3.3 One logical change = one commit, don’t stack them up

The third rule is commit discipline. Without explicit rules, AI tends to dump ten file changes into a single commit. Later when you need to revert a portion of it, everything’s wrapped together and there’s no clean way to separate them.

So spell it out: one logical change = one commit. For example:

  • Add tests for component A → one commit
  • Fix router bug in component B → another commit
  • Refactor shared logic C → another commit

That way reverting any one piece later doesn’t disturb the others. Combined with the rollback strategy from section 5, the cost of recovery stays low.

Commit message template (Conventional Commits)

AI commit cadence prompt template

Tell AI before starting:

4. Wrap-up validation: three safety nets catching AI mistakes

Writing the code isn’t the same as finishing. Wrap-up has three safety nets, each catching a different kind of mistake.

4.1 First net: typecheck + test + build, three validations

Every time a task wraps up and gets delivered, have AI run three validations:

  1. pnpm typecheck (or tsc --noEmit) — confirms types pass
  2. pnpm test — confirms existing tests still pass
  3. pnpm build — confirms a clean production bundle

These three catch different layers:

  • typecheck catches type errors — e.g. AI passing a User object to a function that takes string
  • test catches behavior errors — e.g. AI changed a function and a case that should return true now returns false
  • build catches errors that only show after bundling — e.g. an import path that works in dev mode but blows up in production build

Only running one of these isn’t enough. All three together is a reasonably complete safety net.

Pre-delivery checklist template

4.2 Second net: Writer / Reviewer dual-Claude mode

This is Anthropic’s officially recommended advanced workflow in best practices. Simply put: open two Claude sessions, one writes and one reviews. Why? Claude reviewing code it just wrote suffers from confirmation bias — it can’t catch its own problems easily. A fresh-context Claude catches more edge cases.

How to do it:

Session A (Writer)Session B (Reviewer)
Implement the rate limiter middleware for A component
Review @src/middleware/rateLimiter.ts. Find edge cases, race conditions, inconsistencies with the existing middleware pattern
Apply the review feedback: [Session B output]

A more advanced approach: turn the Reviewer into a sub-agent (like the security-reviewer from section 2.2). Then every time you finish writing, you can fire off a review with one call — no need to manually open a new session.

4.3 Third net: cross-check AI’s cited versions, packages, APIs against the official docs

The third safety net is anti-stale-memory: every time AI cites a package version, an API usage, a cloud console step — anything that updates frequently — open the official docs and verify before letting that memory ship.

Why so paranoid? Because AI’s training data has a cutoff date. Package versions, API specs, cloud console UIs change fast — what AI answers from memory is often several months out of date. It might write API usage for some package that’s been renamed in the new major version. It might tell you to click X in some cloud console, but the console UI’s been redesigned.

AI citation cross-check list

5. Mistake recovery: rollback decision table

In practice, AI is going to mess up code at some point. The most wasteful response when this happens is to keep piling on fixes in the wrong direction — the hole just gets deeper. So you need an explicit rollback judgment rule.

In 2026 Claude Code added a feature called /rewind (or Esc + Esc) — rolls you back to any checkpoint and restores the code state Claude changed. That’s the first layer of recovery.

Note
Important caveat — Anthropic’s docs explicitly say checkpoints only track files Claude changed, not file changes from Bash commands (e.g. node_modules touched by npm install, things deleted by rm, build artifacts). For those, you need git. So git commit discipline can’t be skipped.

The rule in one sentence: was the broken part an execution detail, or the planning direction?

Rollback decision table

SituationAction
AI’s just-finished change doesn’t pass review/rewind (or Esc + Esc) to last checkpoint
Single file’s implementation detail wrong, direction OKgit restore <file> and redo
Multiple related files broken, direction OKgit restore multiple files, redo
AI applied wrong architecture / misread the requirementScrap the plan and rewrite from the planning phase
Tests added don’t cover the implementation intentScrap tests, re-examine the plan, re-add tests
AI changed too much, can’t tell what’s good and what’s badFirst git status to confirm + git stash as insurance, then git restore . to roll everything back (this is why small commits matter)
typecheck / test failing at large scaleHigh chance the direction’s wrong, stop and check the plan

You can see from the table — rollback isn’t failure, it’s part of the workflow. Treat it as a tool for catching AI mistakes and your whole development rhythm gets much steadier.

6. Memory system: two-layer architecture + positive pattern templates

The final piece is memory system design. Once this is in place, all the discipline above becomes things AI does on its own — you don’t have to remind it every time.

Memory has two layers:

6.1 Global layer: ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md or ~/.claude/rules/ for cross-project rules

For rules that should apply across projects — rules you explicitly want Claude to follow every time (package manager conventions, test discipline, no over-engineering, no scope creep) — Anthropic’s docs define two locations:

  • ~/.claude/CLAUDE.md — single file, auto-loaded every session. Good for 5-10 core principles.
  • ~/.claude/rules/<topic>.md — split into per-topic files (one rule per file), also auto-loaded every session. The advantage: you can use YAML frontmatter paths: for path-scoped rules (e.g. a rule that only loads when working in src/api/**)

New projects inherit them automatically — no need to re-teach.

Note
Worth clarifying a common confusion — Claude Code also has a feature called Auto memory, which Claude accumulates on its own (not what you write by hand), stored at ~/.claude/projects/<project>/memory/, scoped per project, not global. The “global rules” in this section refers to specs you write by hand for AI to follow — different thing from Auto memory. For the full mechanism, see What is Claude Code Auto Memory? Complete Guide to Auto Memory.

Global rule template (pnpm convention)

~/.claude/rules/pnpm.md:

Global rule template (test-along discipline)

~/.claude/rules/testing.md:

Global rule template (stay in scope)

~/.claude/rules/minimal-scope.md:

6.2 Project layer: AGENTS.md / CLAUDE.md for project-specific rules

Project layer holds design decisions, product boundaries, and traps specific to this project. The judgment rule: would this rule still apply in a different project? If yes, put it in global. If only in this project, put it in the project layer.

If you want the full Claude Code memory mechanism, see my previous post: What is Claude Code Auto Memory? Complete Guide to Auto Memory.

6.3 When writing rules, positive patterns beat prohibitions

Last key thing: when writing rules, mix prohibitions with positive patterns. Prohibitions alone aren’t enough.

TypeExampleEffect
ProhibitionDon’t lead with bullet outlines, don’t use emojis, don’t over-engineerAI avoids it, but doesn’t know what “right” looks like
Positive patternA good plan doc includes goals, task breakdown, what’s out of scope, and acceptance criteriaAI knows what to actively produce

You can see from the table — prohibitions only block traps. They can’t actively guide good output. So they need positive patterns alongside.

Positive pattern template

~/.claude/rules/plan-template.md:

Use both: prohibitions to block traps, positive patterns to guide.

Real-world case study: a personal e-commerce MVP from spec to ship

After six sections of theory, let’s walk through one realistic case: building a small personal e-commerce MVP — selling handmade goods, and it has to actually take money.

Stack: Nuxt 3 + Vue 3 + Tailwind + Pinia + Supabase (DB + Auth) + Stripe (payment) + Vercel (hosting). This stack is fairly mainstream for 2026 small e-commerce MVPs — no backend to host, payments handled by Stripe, one-click deploy.

Step 1 — Write CLAUDE.md (with AGENTS.md) as the project constitution

First thing in a new project is writing CLAUDE.md (and AGENTS.md too if your team uses multiple tools — CLAUDE.md’s first line is @AGENTS.md to import it). E-commerce has extra emphasis on money / security / RLS compared to a regular tool, so the constitution must spell these out:

Step 2 — Write ROADMAP.md splitting three weeks

docs/ROADMAP.md:

Step 3 — Enter plan mode and discuss with AI before starting

Open Claude Code, enter plan mode:

AI will return a plan roughly like:

  • Supabase schema:
    • products: id, slug, name, description, price_cents, image_url, stock
    • orders: id, stripe_session_id, customer_email, total_cents, status, created_at
    • order_items: id, order_id, product_id, quantity, price_cents_at_purchase
  • Code structure:
    • composables/api/useProducts.ts — products fetch
    • composables/useCart.ts — Pinia store + localStorage sync
    • utils/cart.ts — pure functions (subtotal, shipping, total)
    • pages/index.vue, pages/products/[slug].vue
    • Matching test files
  • RLS policy: products public read, orders only server-side service_role can write

Once the plan checks out, have AI write it to docs/tasks.md, then exit plan mode and start implementing.

Step 4 — Implement + add tests in the same turn

Before starting, prompt:

AI will:

  1. Write utils/cart.ts (pure functions: calculate subtotal, total) → same turn write utils/cart.test.ts → run pnpm test
  2. Write composables/api/useProducts.ts → same turn write matching tests (mock Supabase client)
  3. Write composables/useCart.ts → same turn write tests
  4. Write pages/index.vue + pages/products/[slug].vue UI → call the ui-engineer sub-agent (template from section 2.2) for Tailwind classes

Step 5 — Three validations + Writer/Reviewer to catch security issues

After implementation, run:

Then — this is the most critical step in the e-commerce case — open another Claude session as Reviewer, specifically reviewing the security layer:

Step 6 — Split commits

Follow Conventional Commits, one logical change per commit:

Down the road, if any piece of Week 1 needs reverting, every commit is an independent logical change — you can cleanly extract just that one.

Step 7 — Hitting a trap: frontend price tampering security issue

After wiring up Stripe Checkout in Week 2, run Writer/Reviewer. Reviewer Claude catches a critical issue:

server/api/checkout.post.ts directly takes price_cents from the frontend cart to build the Stripe Session — classic client-side trust vulnerability. A user can open DevTools, change localStorage, turn a 10000 product into a 1 product, and pay.

Run it through the rollback decision table:

SituationDecision
Not a single-field wrong value✗ git restore single file doesn’t fit
Trust boundary in the architecture is wrong✓ Scrap plan, rewrite Stripe portion

So the right move is scrap the plan and rewrite the Stripe checkout flow. New plan:

  1. /api/checkout only accepts product_id and quantity, not price
  2. Server-side queries each product’s price_cents from Supabase
  3. Use server-side computed amount to build the Stripe Checkout Session

After fixing, write this trap into AGENTS.md as a long-term rule:

Also promote it to the global layer in ~/.claude/rules/, since this rule applies to any e-commerce, subscription, or money-handling project:

This dramatically lowers the chance of stepping on the same trap in Week 3 admin / future refund flow / future subscription mechanism. That’s the full demo of the meta-pattern, trap-to-SOP: caught by review → scrap plan and rewrite → write into AGENTS.md + global ~/.claude/rules/ → dramatically lower the re-trip probability (not 100% immune — AI occasionally still misses rules — but the frequency drops sharply).

Where this SOP doesn’t work: limits and failure modes

Time to be honest about limits — this SOP isn’t a silver bullet. A few rounds in, you’ll hit these enemies:

  1. Overkill for small projects: weekend demo, one-shot script, PoC to test the waters — maintaining the full CLAUDE.md + AGENTS.md + ROADMAP + tasks.md + sub-agent configuration is heavy. Vibe coding’s fine here — the “when to use” column in the earlier table already says so.
  2. Token costs aren’t cheap: Writer/Reviewer means running Claude twice per review. Sub-agents are fresh-context every call. With Opus heavy usage, monthly bills hit three-digit USD easily. A leaner config: heavy tasks (plan, implement) on Opus, lighter tasks (Reviewer, test-writer, ui-engineer sub-agents) on Sonnet or Haiku.
  3. Context decay is this SOP’s biggest enemy: past 100K+ tokens of conversation, AI starts dropping rules, forgetting CLAUDE.md discipline, and forgetting to sync tasks.md. Build the habit of running /compact regularly and opening new sessions when needed — don’t let one conversation run too long.
  4. Sub-agent routing isn’t reliably stable: Claude’s auto-detection of “should I invoke ui-engineer for this task” isn’t 100% accurate. Over time the main agent picks up Tailwind work itself. The reliable approach is to name the sub-agent explicitly in the prompt — don’t rely entirely on auto-trigger.
  5. More memory isn’t always better: past 20-30 entries, AI won’t follow every single one. Important rules and minor rules get diluted when mixed. Prune regularly — drop outdated or low-priority entries instead of always adding new ones.

These limits aren’t reasons not to use the SOP. They’re context for setting realistic expectations. The SOP raises stability — it doesn’t guarantee stability. What’s actually stable is the underlying Git, tests, typecheck, build, and human review. AI just makes you faster.

Wrapping up: make this SOP your own

Recap — what this post gives you is a complete agentic engineering workflow from spec to ship:

  1. Planning phase: CLAUDE.md (with AGENTS.md) as project constitution + ROADMAP.md (feature plan) + tasks.md (progress sync), tied together with @<path> import
  2. Division of labor: Skill injected into main context, Sub-agent with isolated context, Agent Teams running in parallel
  3. Development discipline: tests in the same turn + UI delegated to specialists + one logical change per commit
  4. Wrap-up validation: typecheck + test + build three validations + Writer/Reviewer dual-Claude + AI citation cross-check
  5. Mistake recovery: /rewind → git restore → scrap plan and rewrite, three layers of recovery
  6. Memory system: global + project two-layer architecture + positive pattern templates

There’s a common spirit running through all six sections: every single rule is a discipline against AI amnesia, direction drift, and side effects. Karpathy’s core point about agentic engineering is exactly this — you’re no longer the engineer writing code by hand, you’re the engineer supervising a team of AI employees. Your work is designing specs, supervising plans, reviewing diffs, writing tests, building evals, managing permissions.

More importantly, once you start running this workflow, you’ll gradually hit traps unique to your project. When you do, don’t just solve them in the moment — write them into memory or CLAUDE.md / AGENTS.md to make them next time’s rule. Same trap never gets hit twice. Every trap caught becomes another step of solidified workflow. Eventually you end up with an agentic engineering SOP that’s genuinely yours.

Hope this helps you build your own agentic engineering workflow. If you run a few rounds and your workflow diverges from mine, that’s totally normal — every team has different habits. Pick the parts that work for you, try them out, and slowly tune your own version.
entry.summary: I've written about what Vibe Coding is and the basics newcomers should pick up. This post is the 2026 upgrade — the full playbook. In April 2026 Karpathy himself, at the Sequoia Ascent talk, upgraded vibe coding into agentic engineering. So this post takes the agentic engineering discipline and rebuilds the AI development workflow from scratch — three-layer documentation (Spec / Plan / Tasks via CLAUDE.md + AGENTS.md, ROADMAP, tasks.md templates), AI employee division of labor (Skill vs Sub-agent vs Agent Teams), development discipline (tests in the same turn, UI delegation, Conventional Commits), two-layer wrap-up validation (typecheck + test + build, plus Writer/Reviewer mode and AI citation cross-checks), a rollback decision table, and a two-tier Memory + CLAUDE.md architecture. The whole thing runs end-to-end against a personal e-commerce MVP (Nuxt 3 + Supabase + Stripe), including how to defuse the classic Stripe-checkout client-price-tampering security trap.

entry.title: Fixing 'Blocked request' When Exposing a Nuxt 3 Dev Server Through ngrok
entry.content:
Intro

If you’ve been using ngrok to expose your local dev server, after upgrading to Nuxt 3 (or bumping Vite past 6.x), you’ve probably hit this message:

My first reaction was to do a double take — isn’t Nuxt running on Nitro? Why is it telling me to edit vite.config.js? Then it clicked — Nuxt 3’s client side actually runs on Vite, and this host check is a security measure Vite added in 6.x to prevent DNS Rebinding attacks.

But Nuxt 3 isn’t a pure Vite project, so there’s no vite.config.js to edit. This post is about where to actually put the config. While I’m at it, I previously wrote Solving CORS in Nuxt 3 with Nitro Proxy, which also covers Nuxt 3 dev server config — pair them together if useful.

How to fix it

Just add server.allowedHosts under the vite key in nuxt.config.ts:

Nuxt 3’s config all lives in nuxt.config.ts, with Vite-related options tucked under the vite key. So when the error tells you to edit vite.config.js, for a Nuxt project that means this spot. Save it, restart pnpm dev, and open the ngrok URL again — should work.

Wildcard once and for all

ngrok’s free tier gives you a new subdomain every time you restart the tunnel — so you don’t want to come back and edit this every time.

Vite has a wildcard syntax for this — a leading . on the string covers all subdomains:

Now whatever new URL ngrok hands you, it’s allowed. For me, I add .ngrok-free.app, .ngrok.app, and .ngrok.io so I don’t have to come back and edit when I eventually switch to a paid plan.

If you also use tunnels like Cloudflare Tunnel or Tailscale Funnel, add their domains to the same array.

Why not just allow everything?

Vite has a more brute-force option — set allowedHosts to true to allow everything:

Note
I really don’t like writing it this way — it nukes the whole point of Vite’s host check. Yes, the dev server only runs locally, but once you’ve fired up ngrok or Cloudflare Tunnel, your dev server is effectively public. Spending ten more seconds on a whitelist is worth it.

Wrap-up

Quick summary:

  • Nuxt 3 has no vite.config.js — Vite config goes under the vite key in nuxt.config.ts
  • allowedHosts takes an array of allowed hosts; a leading . is the wildcard for subdomains
  • Don’t just set allowedHosts: true for convenience — listing a whitelist is the smarter call
  • This setting only affects the dev server; production builds aren’t touched

Hope this helps if you got blocked by ngrok + Nuxt 3. If you’re also wading through Nuxt 3 setup issues, my earlier post Fixing ‘Issues with peer dependencies found’ Warning When Initializing a Nuxt 3 Project might be useful.

References

  • Vite｜server.allowedHosts
  • Nuxt 3｜Configuration - vite
entry.summary: A quick note on fixing "Blocked request. This host is not allowed" when exposing a Nuxt 3 dev server through ngrok — including why Nuxt points you at a `vite.config.js` that doesn't exist, where to actually put `server.allowedHosts` in `nuxt.config.ts`, how to use the leading-dot wildcard to cover all ngrok subdomains, and why setting `allowedHosts: true` is a bad idea.

entry.title: No Preview Image When You Share to LINE or Discord? Force-Refresh Social OG Cache with ?v=1
entry.content:
Intro

This post starts with the classic problem of sharing your blog post to a social platform and getting a stale preview image, then walks through Open Graph tags, how platforms cache og:image / og:title / og:description, why Facebook / X / LINE / Discord still show old content after you update your OG tags, and how to force a refresh by appending a cache-busting query string like ?v=1 ?v=2. Also covers how to use the Facebook Sharing Debugger, rough cache lifetimes by platform, what to do about Twitter / X’s Card Validator being retired in 2022, and whether ?v=1 impacts SEO or canonical URLs.

If you’ve been playing with Cloudflare Pages deployments or trying to lock down preview URLs recently, you can pair this with Cloudflare Pages Preview URLs Stay Live Forever? Lock Them Down with Cloudflare Access.

The story

A few days ago I wrote a post about using Cloudflare Access to lock down preview URLs. Once it was deployed, I excitedly shared the URL to LINE for a friend to read — and the share card came back without a cover image. Just the default placeholder octocat showing up as the preview thumbnail.

That was weird, because the article has a cover image right at the top, and og:image should’ve picked it up. I went back to inspect the blog HTML — og:image was pointing at the right cover image, curl-ing the image URL returned 200 OK, but LINE just wouldn’t pick it up.

Tried Discord, tried X — same default image everywhere. I started laughing at myself. The article was clearly fine, but every social platform was stuck on stale data.

After some digging, I realized this isn’t a blog problem at all — it’s the social platforms’ OG cache doing its thing.

Why do social platforms cache the preview?

Bottom line up front: social platforms cache each URL’s OG metadata, and a second post of the same URL won’t refetch from your site — it just pulls from cache.

OG stands for Open Graph, a metadata spec proposed by Facebook in 2010. It lets social platforms quickly grab a link’s title, description, and preview image. Sites just put a few meta tags in <head> and social platforms pick them up:

So why cache? Basically cost savings.

Think about it: you post a link, 100 friends see that post. The platform isn’t going to hit your site 100 times — that’d be 100 requests. So the first time anyone posts the URL, the platform fetches OG metadata once and stores it; everyone else who sees the post (or reposts the same URL later) reads from cache. Your site doesn’t get hit again.

That design is reasonable on its own, but when you’re a content creator who just updated your og:image and want the preview to refresh, cache becomes the obstacle.

Rough cache durations per platform:

PlatformCache expiryOfficial debugger?
Facebook~7–30 daysYes (Sharing Debugger)
LinkedIn~7 daysYes (Post Inspector)
X (formerly Twitter)~24–48 hoursPreview function discontinued
Discord~30 minutesNone
LINENot officially publishedNone
Slack / TelegramNot officially publishedNone

Discord has the shortest cache. Facebook and LinkedIn are on the longer end. LINE / Slack / Telegram have never published official numbers, so when you run into stale previews there, you’re stuck using the workaround below.

Forcing a refresh with ?v=1

The simplest way to force a refresh: append a query string, like:

To the social cache, different URL strings are different entries. The original https://israynotarray.com/other/20260513/8264193057/ is cached, but adding ?v=1 makes it a “new URL” — cache miss — and the platform refetches your latest OG metadata.

The number after v= is arbitrary. Next time you want to force-refresh, use v=2, v=3 — any value you haven’t posted before. It doesn’t even have to be called v — ?refresh=1, ?nocache=true also work, as long as it’s a query string the cache hasn’t seen.

Your site won’t break from ?v=1

You might worry: will ?v=1 cause issues on my site?

Don’t worry — most frameworks and web servers ignore unknown query strings by default. Hexo, Next.js, Vite, Cloudflare Pages, Vercel, etc., all serve the original path content when they get ?v=1. Readers see exactly the same page, no difference at all.

Note
If your site already uses query strings for something (like pagination ?page=2 or search ?q=xxx), just avoid colliding with your existing query keys. ?v, ?refresh, ?nocache are safe bets — extremely unlikely to clash with anything your site already does.

What works per platform

Facebook: use the Sharing Debugger

Facebook is the only platform that still has a working official debugger: https://developers.facebook.com/tools/debug/. Paste your URL, click Debug to see what FB sees, and if it’s stale, click Scrape Again to force a refetch.

The Sharing Debugger also tells you if og:image has problems (too large, unsupported format, unfetchable, etc.) — really helpful for debugging.

Note
As of 2026, Facebook’s official docs still only support JPG, PNG, and GIF for og:image — WebP is not supported. If you use a WebP image as og:image, FB will break. Use PNG or JPG for og:image, and reserve WebP for the rest of your site for performance.

Also, og:image’s recommended size is 1200 x 630 pixels. Anything below 600 x 315 gets shown as a small thumbnail by Facebook, or sometimes not shown at all.

Sometimes the first Scrape Again doesn’t work — clicking two or three times in a row often does. There’s no rational explanation for this. FB just gets weird sometimes.

X, LinkedIn, LINE, Discord: just use ?v=1

X’s Card Validator pulled the preview function in 2022, and the remaining pages keep degrading. LinkedIn has Post Inspector, but it’s more limited. LINE, Discord, Slack, Telegram have never offered a debugger.

So for these platforms, the fastest and most universal solution is ?v=1 cache busting. Next time you’re pasting a URL into a LINE group, slap a ?v=1 on the end first — the platform refetches your site and grabs the fresh OG.

Note
Already-shared messages in the group won’t update — social caches bind to the message that captured them. Whatever the old message has, it’ll keep showing. To fix, delete the original message and repost with ?v=1.

Alternative: third-party OG preview tools

If you want to check what the preview will look like before posting, there are also third-party tools that fetch your page’s OG in real time:

  • opengraph.dev
  • share-preview.com
  • microlink.io

These pull your OG meta live, with no relationship to any platform’s cache, so they’re useful for checking og:image works before publishing. But — a third-party tool succeeding doesn’t guarantee FB / LINE will also succeed. Trust the official debugger (FB Sharing Debugger) as the source of truth.

Does this affect SEO?

No, assuming your site has canonical URLs set up properly.

<link rel="canonical"> is a tag in <head> that tells search engines “this is the canonical URL for this page“. When Google fetches https://your-site.com/post/?v=1 and finds:

It treats ?v=1 as the same page as the original. Search rank doesn’t get split, and it doesn’t get flagged as duplicate content.

On top of that, ?v=1 is something you only use occasionally to force a social cache refresh — you’re not actively promoting that variant URL. In practice, Google probably never even sees it. Zero real SEO impact.

Note
If you’re not sure whether your site has a canonical URL, open an article, hit F12 → Elements, and look for <link rel="canonical"> — check whether the href matches the URL you’d expect. Most modern blog frameworks (Hexo, Astro, Next.js, Vite SSG) add it automatically.

Bonus: always test before posting

One habit I picked up after getting bitten enough times: run the URL through FB Sharing Debugger before posting.

Workflow is straightforward:

  1. After publishing, open Facebook Sharing Debugger.
  2. Paste your article URL, click Debug.
  3. Check that og:image, og:title, og:description are correct.
  4. If they’re wrong, fix them on your site and click Scrape Again.
  5. Once everything’s clean, share to your social platforms.

It adds a step, but it’s way better than posting first, realizing the preview is broken, having to delete the message, having to ask friends to click again — that 1 minute upfront pays for itself.

Wrapping up

Social caching is a good thing most of the time (saves your site bandwidth, makes previews snappy), but the moment you need to update og:image, it becomes the obstacle. The ?v=1 trick feels kind of low-tech, but it genuinely works — especially on debugger-less platforms like LINE and Discord, where it’s basically the only option.

Hopefully this gives you a clearer picture of social OG caching and cache-busting. If you’re running your blog on Hexo or Cloudflare Pages, my previous post Cloudflare Pages Preview URLs Stay Live Forever? Lock Them Down with Cloudflare Access pairs nicely with this one — together they cover both the deployment-side gotchas and the sharing-side ones. See you in the next post.
entry.summary: This post starts with the classic problem of sharing your blog post to a social platform and getting a stale preview image, then walks through Open Graph tags, how platforms cache og:image / og:title / og:description, why Facebook / X / LINE / Discord still show old content after you update your OG tags, and how to force a refresh by appending a cache-busting query string like `?v=1` `?v=2`. Also covers how to use the Facebook Sharing Debugger, rough cache lifetimes by platform, what to do about Twitter / X's Card Validator being retired in 2022, and whether `?v=1` impacts SEO or canonical URLs.

entry.title: Cloudflare Pages Preview URLs Stay Live Forever? Lock Them Down with Cloudflare Access
entry.content:
Intro

Every Cloudflare Pages deployment gets a hash-based preview URL like d682e28c.your-project.pages.dev. This post starts with the security problem of those URLs persisting forever, then walks through how to use Cloudflare Access (free for up to 50 users) to put a Self-hosted application policy in front of them — locking the previews behind an “Emails ending in” rule. Covers the step-by-step setup, how to write OR rules in Access policies, and three things to check when the One-time PIN email doesn’t arrive.

If you don’t have a Cloudflare domain or Pages project yet, you might want to read Google Domains shutting down? Complete walkthrough for setting up a domain and deploying with Cloudflare (article in Mandarin) first — it has the full Pages deployment flow and pairs nicely with this one.

The story

I’ve been playing with Hexo blog auto-deployment lately, wiring the project up to Cloudflare Pages so every git push triggers a new deploy. Cloudflare is nice about it — beyond the production your-project.pages.dev URL, every deployment also gets its own preview URL:

That d682e28c is the first 8 chars of the commit hash. It’s a super useful mechanism — you can share it with a colleague or a teacher to show “here’s what this version looks like”, without merging to main first.

But then something hit me: how long does that preview URL stay live? If the link ever got shared by accident, could it become a security problem? Some companies preview sensitive content on these (internal projects, unreleased features) — if the preview URLs hang around forever, that’s basically a permanent snapshot just sitting there, waiting for someone to dig it up.

I went and looked it up… yep. Forever.

How long do Cloudflare Pages preview URLs last?

Like I said: forever.

The official docs for Preview deployments spell it out pretty clearly:

When a preview deployment is published, it is given a unique, hash-based address — for example, <hash>.<project>.pages.dev. These are atomic and may always be visited in the future.

Translation: every preview deployment gets a hash-based URL, that URL is atomic (immutable), and it can always be accessed in the future.

In other words, unless you manually delete that deployment from the Cloudflare dashboard, that URL stays accessible forever, showing whatever you pushed that time.

Note
Not only does Cloudflare not auto-clean old previews — per the official docs, once a Pages project hits 100+ deployments, even deleting the whole project fails. You have to clean up old deployments via Wrangler first.

So what’s the risk?

The hash has 8 random hex characters, so brute-forcing one is impractical — these URLs aren’t going to get picked up by random scanners.

But that doesn’t mean there’s no risk. A few common scenarios:

  • You paste the preview link into a Slack or Discord group.
  • You send the link to a designer, PM, or teacher.
  • You tweet it out or screenshot it into a blog post.
  • You demo to a client and forget to delete it after the meeting.

If any of these end up in front of someone who shouldn’t see it, you can’t take the URL back. Even if you later delete the branch or rename the project, that preview deployment URL stays alive. That’s what bugs me about it.

So here’s the approach I use: put Cloudflare Access in front of the preview URL, so only authorized people can get in.

Locking previews with Cloudflare Access

Cloudflare Access is a ZTNA (Zero Trust Network Access) service under Cloudflare Zero Trust. Its job is to put an authentication gate in front of your site — anyone trying to get in has to authenticate first.

Does it cost money? Per the official Cloudflare Zero Trust pricing page, the Zero Trust Free plan supports up to 50 users for free. For a personal blog, that’s basically free-tier territory.

Step 1: Create a Self-hosted application

Go to the Cloudflare dashboard and find Zero Trust in the left sidebar. (First time here, you’ll be asked to pick a Team name — that becomes your login URL https://your-team-name.cloudflareaccess.com.)

Then follow this path:

In the destination section, fill in:

FieldWhat to enter
Subdomain*
DomainYour Pages domain, e.g. your-project.pages.dev
Pathleave blank

Setting subdomain to * is the key part — that way <any hash>.your-project.pages.dev all get covered by this application, and the production your-project.pages.dev is protected too.

Step 2: Configure an Access policy

Next, decide who’s allowed in. There’s an Access Policies block below:

Click Create new policy and fill in:

FieldWhat to enter
Policy namee.g. Internal only
ActionAllow
Rule selectorEmails ending in
Value@gmail.com (your company domain)

If you also want your personal gmail to be able to log in, add another rule under the same policy:

RuleSelectorValue
Rule 1Emails ending in@gmail.com
Rule 2Emailsyour-name@gmail.com

The two rules are OR’d together — either condition true is enough.

Step 3: Save the application

Last, fill in the application name and session duration (I’d suggest 24 hours), hit Create, done.

Now open an incognito window, paste your preview URL — you should see Cloudflare’s login page asking for an email. Enter a valid one, you get a PIN email, enter the PIN, and you’re in.

Set everything up but the PIN email isn’t coming?

If you’re like me, set everything up excitedly, and then the PIN email just… doesn’t arrive — stay calm. Three places to check.

First: spam folder. The Cloudflare PIN email is sent from noreply@notify.cloudflare.com, and Gmail very often files it under Spam or the Promotions tab. Subject looks something like Your one-time code is.... Check both folders.

Second: the email you entered doesn’t match the policy. This is the sneaky one. If your policy says “ends in @hexschool.com“ but you logged in with gmail.com, Cloudflare silently denies — doesn’t send the email, doesn’t tell you why. Just silence.

Note
From a security perspective this design makes sense (don’t tell attackers which emails are whitelisted), but for someone setting this up the first time it’s incredibly confusing. Double-check the email you entered actually matches the policy.

Third: One-time PIN isn’t enabled. Go to Zero Trust → Settings → Authentication → Login methods and confirm One-time PIN is active. If not, hit Add to enable it.

Bonus: bulk-delete old previews with Wrangler

If your goal isn’t “block people” but “just nuke the old previews entirely”, you can use Cloudflare’s Wrangler CLI to list everything:

Combined with wrangler pages deployment delete <id> to delete one at a time, this is way faster than clicking through the dashboard. As mentioned earlier, once you’ve got 100+ deployments, even deleting the whole project fails — this command becomes essential for maintenance.

Wrapping up

Cloudflare Pages previews are double-edged: super convenient when you want to share, slightly scary when you forget the URL is permanent. Before writing this post I was naively treating preview URLs as “temporary test environments” — looking back, they’re permanent snapshots just sitting there waiting to be discovered.

Hopefully this gives you a clearer picture of how Cloudflare Pages previews work and how Access can lock them down. If you’re moving your blog onto Cloudflare too, my older post Cache Firebase Storage images with Cloudflare Workers for free (article in Mandarin) is another piece of the Cloudflare deploy story. See you in the next post.
entry.summary: Every Cloudflare Pages deployment gets a hash-based preview URL like `d682e28c.your-project.pages.dev`. This post starts with the security problem of those URLs persisting forever, then walks through how to use Cloudflare Access (free for up to 50 users) to put a Self-hosted application policy in front of them — locking the previews behind an "Emails ending in" rule. Covers the step-by-step setup, how to write OR rules in Access policies, and three things to check when the One-time PIN email doesn't arrive.

entry.title: What's RSS? Why Still Use It in 2026? Plus the Chrome Extension I Wrote — Raydar
entry.content:
Intro

This post walks through what RSS actually is, how it differs from Newsletter subscriptions, why it’s still worth using in 2026, how it compares to algorithm-driven feeds, what Atom is — and then I talk about Raydar, a Chrome RSS reader extension I wrote. It covers RSS auto-detection, one-click subscribe, three reading modes, local-first storage, no account required, OPML import/export, JSON backup, keyboard shortcuts — plus how to install it and why I bothered writing my own.

The story

I’ve been wrapping up a Chrome extension I’ve been writing — an RSS reader called Raydar.

After finishing, I mentioned it offhand to a friend, and got back: “What’s RSS?” That made me realize something. RSS is well known in dev circles, but for the general public the term hasn’t been visible in years.

On top of that, after Google Reader shut down in 2013, plenty of articles wrote that “RSS is dead”, so a lot of newer folks just skip past it, assuming it’s from a previous era.

But honestly, in an era where algorithms are getting more aggressive, IG / X keep reranking your feed, and news sites are paywalling more aggressively, RSS is one of the few remaining ways to actually decide what you see. So this post is about what RSS is, why it’s still worth using, and then a little about the small tool I wrote.

What is RSS, really?

In short: RSS is a website’s publicly-published “latest content list”, in XML format, containing each article’s title, link, publish date, summary, and so on.

What does that look like? An example helps.

If you open my blog’s homepage, you see a page laid out for human eyes — navbar, sidebar, ads, interactive bits.

If you instead open https://israynotarray.com/atom.xml, you see something like this:

That’s RSS. (Strictly this snippet is Atom, but ignore that for now — I’ll cover the difference later.)

It’s the machine-readable version of the site — no CSS, no JavaScript, no ads, just a clean content listing. RSS readers periodically fetch these XML files and aggregate the latest items from all your subscriptions into one place.

An everyday analogy

If I had to describe RSS in non-tech terms, it’s like “newspaper subscriptions, but reversed”.

Traditional newspaper subscriptions: the publisher delivers to your home. RSS: you go check the publisher’s door for “anything new?”, and grab it if there is. The difference is you don’t need to give the publisher your address (email) — you pull, they don’t know who you are.

That “you pull, they don’t know who you are” property is what makes RSS worth revisiting in this era of pervasive tracking.

How does RSS differ from newsletter subscriptions?

Newsletters have been hot for the last few years — Substack, ConvertKit, etc. — so I get asked a lot whether RSS and newsletters are the same.

They’re not. The differences are pretty big:

AspectRSSNewsletter
DeliveryYou pullThey push to your inbox
Required infoNoneYour email
PrivacyThey don’t know who you areThey have your email and can track opens
UnsubscribeJust remove from your readerHave to find the unsubscribe link; sometimes ignored
ContentPure content, no trackingOften has tracking pixels, UTM params
AggregationOne reader sees everythingInbox is mixed with personal mail

You can see they take pretty different approaches. Newsletters’ upside is the push model and the closeness between author and reader. The downsides are inbox overload and your email being held by many parties.

RSS goes the other way: you decide when to pull, what to pull, what to read with. It’s the more “data ownership stays with me” approach.

Why use RSS in 2026?

This part is more personal opinion, but a few reasons made me actually go back to RSS in recent years.

Algorithms have eaten most of your attention

You’ve probably hit this: open IG, scroll for 20 minutes, realize you’ve been watching videos from people you don’t follow. Open X to check someone’s latest post, the home feed is all recommendations. Open YouTube to look at your subscriptions, the home page recommendations outweigh actual subscriptions.

This isn’t an illusion — it’s the design.

Platforms want you on the platform as long as possible, so they push “things we think you’ll keep watching” to you, and what you actually subscribed to gets pushed way down the priority list.

RSS doesn’t do this. Whoever you subscribed to is who you see, sorted by publish date, newest to oldest. Clean.

You stop missing the things you actually care about

Following on from the last point — because algorithms “filter” for you, the niche sources you actually care about get hidden.

For me personally, I subscribe to a few personal blogs that publish maybe once a month. Authors like that can’t compete with algorithm-driven feeds on IG or Twitter, but in an RSS reader, when they publish, it shows up in my timeline. Doesn’t get buried.

One place for everything

Tech blogs, news sites, open-source project releases, Hacker News, specific subreddits, GitHub trending, even Mastodon and Bluesky — all of it can flow through RSS. Once you get used to it, you stop needing five separate apps. One reader handles everything.

AI-era data sources matter more, not less

This one doesn’t come up much.

Training AI models tends to lean heavily on “structured, semantically clear” content. Sites that are RSS-friendly tend to also be semantically clean. So as you start using AI to summarize and process information, RSS feeds become a particularly clean source to point AI tools at.

In other words, RSS isn’t just for human reading anymore — if you’re going to wire AI into your information workflow, RSS is the easiest entry point.

What is Atom, and how is it different from RSS?

Quick aside on Atom.

Atom and RSS are two different specs, but they do essentially the same job — publish a site’s latest content as XML. The differences:

  • RSS (especially 2.0): older, looser field definitions, so different sites produce slightly different formats.
  • Atom: came later, tightened up the spec — for example, date fields are required to be RFC 3339 format.

End-user wise, it doesn’t matter. Modern RSS readers handle both, and sites typically offer both. Your reader figures it out for you.

Note
Besides RSS and Atom, there’s also a much older format called RDF (RSS 1.0), but it’s rare to encounter in the wild now.

What RSS readers are out there?

Enough about the spec — let’s talk tools. There are quite a few options:

  • Feedly: probably the best-known cloud RSS reader. Free tier is limited.
  • Inoreader: another cloud reader, a bit more featured than Feedly.
  • NetNewsWire: native macOS / iOS app, open source, free.
  • Reeder: longstanding paid app on Apple platforms.
  • Miniflux: self-hostable open-source reader, minimalist.

These are all solid in their own ways, but after trying a bunch I noticed a few shared issues:

  1. Cloud services all require an account, and your subscription data lives on their servers.
  2. Either you pay for the full feature set or you have to self-host.
  3. Native desktop apps don’t have a Chrome-extension experience for “I’m browsing, I want to subscribe to this site I just found”.

So I figured I’d just write my own.

My Chrome extension: Raydar

Raydar is a Chrome extension I’ve been writing the past few months. The goal: a local-first, no-account-required minimal RSS reader, stripping away the “register first, use later” friction that cloud readers all have.

It’s up on the Chrome Web Store: Raydar — Chrome Web Store.

A few features I personally find interesting:

RSS auto-detection while you browse

This was on the design doc from day one. You’ve probably hit a case where you find a great article on some blog, want to subscribe, and can’t find the RSS link anywhere.

Raydar auto-scans any page you’re on for <link rel="alternate"> tags pointing at RSS / Atom, and if it finds one, pops up a subscribe prompt — one click and you’re subscribed. No digging through source code or guessing URLs.

If a site doesn’t expose that link tag, you can also manually paste the feed URL from the toolbar popup.

Three reading modes

Reading preferences vary, so Raydar gives you three:

  • Reader mode: extracts the article body into a clean layout with adjustable font size and theme.
  • Web mode: embeds the original page inside the reading pane — no need to leave the tab.
  • Raw RSS mode: shows the feed’s raw XML, for developers debugging.

I personally use reader mode most, because a lot of news sites have such busy layouts that reader mode is the only way to actually focus on the content.

Fully local-first, no signup

Raydar doesn’t need an account, email, or password. Install and use.
